questions.stem:
A 58-year-old woman with multiple myeloma is midway through a cycle of myelosuppressive chemotherapy. Over several weeks her hemoglobin has declined gradually from 11.2 g/dL to 6.8 g/dL. On evaluation she is afebrile with normal vital signs, ambulates without dyspnea, and has no evidence of active bleeding or cardiac ischemia. As part of a concurrent blood utilization review, the transfusion medicine service is asked why she has remained clinically stable despite this hemoglobin level and whether an RBC transfusion is indicated.

Which of the following best explains her tolerance of this hemoglobin level and is most consistent with evidence-based blood utilization review criteria for a stable patient?

options.text: Gradual, chronic anemia allows compensatory mechanisms—increased cardiac output, increased RBC 2,3-DPG causing a rightward shift of the oxyhemoglobin dissociation curve, and redistribution of blood flow to vital organs—to maintain tissue oxygen delivery; review criteria for stable patients typically use a hemoglobin threshold around 7 g/dL rather than transfusing by number alone
Chemotherapy has decreased her RBC 2,3-DPG, shifting the oxyhemoglobin curve to the left and increasing hemoglobin's affinity for oxygen, which improves oxygen delivery to her tissues
Because her hemoglobin is below 7 g/dL, prospective review criteria mandate immediate transfusion regardless of her clinical status
Transfusing RBC units now would immediately normalize tissue oxygen delivery because stored red cells have elevated 2,3-DPG levels compared to native red cells
This scenario should instead be evaluated by retrospective review, since retrospective review is the appropriate model for real-time transfusion decisions

questions.explanation_correct: In gradually developing (chronic) anemia, the body has time to activate compensatory physiologic mechanisms: increased cardiac output, increased RBC 2,3-DPG (which shifts the oxyhemoglobin dissociation curve to the right and facilitates oxygen release to tissues), and redistribution of blood flow to vital organs. These adaptations allow a hemodynamically stable, asymptomatic patient to tolerate a hemoglobin level that would be poorly tolerated if it fell acutely. This physiology underlies evidence-based blood utilization review criteria, which use thresholds such as hemoglobin <7 g/dL for stable patients as a guideline for evaluating transfusion appropriateness rather than transfusing reflexively by number.

option_explanations.A: Correct. Increased cardiac output, a 2,3-DPG-mediated right shift of the oxyhemoglobin curve (facilitating O2 release), and blood flow redistribution are the source-described compensatory mechanisms for anemia, and they explain why a stable, asymptomatic patient can be managed per restrictive review thresholds rather than transfused automatically.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect and directionally reversed: decreased 2,3-DPG causes a LEFT shift, which increases oxygen affinity and impairs (not improves) oxygen release to tissues. This is the opposite of the physiologic right-shift compensation that actually occurs in chronic anemia.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: a hemoglobin threshold used in blood utilization review criteria (e.g., <7 g/dL for stable patients) is a guideline for evaluating whether transfusion is justified, not an automatic mandate that overrides clinical assessment of a stable, asymptomatic patient.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect and factually backward: stored RBC units have DECREASED 2,3-DPG (a recognized storage lesion), which causes a left shift and can transiently impair, not improve, oxygen unloading after transfusion.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect: retrospective review occurs days to weeks after transfusion and is best suited for large-scale trending and benchmarking, not real-time decisions. Prospective (pre-issue) or concurrent (within 12-24 hours) review models are used for timely transfusion decisions like this one.

questions.teaching_point: Gradual, chronic anemia (as commonly seen with myelosuppressive chemotherapy in oncology patients) triggers physiologic compensation—increased cardiac output, a 2,3-DPG-driven rightward shift of the oxyhemoglobin dissociation curve enhancing tissue O2 release, and blood flow redistribution—that can preserve adequate oxygenation despite a low hemoglobin. Evidence-based blood utilization review uses hemoglobin thresholds (e.g., <7 g/dL in stable patients) as guidance for evaluating transfusion appropriateness, not as an automatic transfusion trigger; clinicians must also recall that stored RBC units carry a storage-lesion-related decrease (not increase) in 2,3-DPG.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 46 - Oxygen Transport & Adaptations
source_references.supports: Compensatory mechanisms for anemia (increased cardiac output, increased 2,3-DPG, blood flow redistribution) and the mechanics of right/left oxyhemoglobin curve shifts, including that stored blood has decreased 2,3-DPG causing a left shift.

source_references.supports: Blood utilization audit criteria often use a hemoglobin threshold such as <7 g/dL for stable patients, and defines prospective (real-time, pre-issue), concurrent (within 12-24 hours), and retrospective (days-weeks later, for trending) review models.

source_references.supports: Anemia in Oncology is listed as a subdomain topic within the Clinical Practice domain, establishing the blueprint linkage for this vignette.

review.notes: All load-bearing facts verified verbatim in SRC-002: Node 46 confirms compensatory mechanisms (increased cardiac output, increased 2,3-DPG causing a right shift that facilitates O2 release, blood flow redistribution) and confirms stored blood has DECREASED 2,3-DPG (left shift), which correctly invalidates options B and D (both reverse the documented physiology). Node 49 confirms audit/review criteria "often utilizing thresholds like Hgb <7 g/dL for stable patients" and correctly defines prospective (real-time/pre-issue), concurrent (12-24h), and retrospective (days-weeks, trending) review mode

questions.stem: A 58-year-old man with acute myeloid leukemia undergoing induction chemotherapy becomes refractory to platelet transfusions. On two consecutive transfusions of ABO-identical apheresis platelets from random donors, the 1-hour corrected count increment (CCI) is below 5,000/µL. Solid-phase multiantigen-bead flow cytometric testing demonstrates broad antibody reactivity against HLA Class I (A and B) antigens, and subsequent platelet units are selected by virtual crossmatch (avoiding donors carrying the corresponding HLA antigens). Despite receiving these HLA-selected units, his posttransfusion platelet increments remain poor. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the antibody workup?

options.text: Repeat solid-phase HLA Class I antibody testing to reassess the patient's current panel-reactive antibody level
Test the patient's serum for antibodies against HLA Class II antigens (HLA-DR, -DQ, -DP), since these are expressed on the platelet surface
Test the patient's serum for platelet-specific (HPA) antibodies, such as anti-HPA-1a
Attribute the poor increments to nonimmune consumption and evaluate the patient for sepsis or disseminated intravascular coagulation
Reconfirm that ABO-identical platelet units were transfused, since ABO mismatch is the most likely explanation for the continued poor increments

questions.explanation_correct: This patient has laboratory-confirmed immune platelet refractoriness (poor 1-hour CCI on two consecutive transfusions) with documented HLA Class I antibodies, and he remains refractory even after HLA-directed (virtual-crossmatch-selected) platelet support. The sources specify that when refractoriness persists despite HLA-compatible units, the next step is to test for platelet-specific (HPA) antibodies, such as anti-HPA-1a; if identified, antigen-negative HPA-selected units should then be sought.

option_explanations.A: Incorrect. The HLA Class I antibody specificity has already been characterized and used to select antigen-negative donors via virtual crossmatch; simply repeating the same HLA assay does not address why HLA-selected platelets are still failing and is not the workup step the sources describe for this situation.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect - a classic board trap. Platelets do NOT express HLA Class II antigens; antibodies to HLA Class II indicate sensitization to contaminating leukocytes in the product, not a mechanism of platelet destruction, so this testing would not explain the continued refractoriness.
option_explanations.C: Correct. Per the refractoriness workup, when HLA-compatible/HLA-selected platelets still fail to produce adequate increments, testing for platelet-specific (HPA) antibodies (e.g., anti-HPA-1a) is indicated, with antigen-negative HPA-selected platelets provided if such antibodies are found.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect - a classic board trap. Nonimmune consumption (e.g., sepsis/DIC) typically produces an acceptable 1-hour count with decline by 24 hours; here the 1-hour CCI itself is poor on repeated occasions, a pattern consistent with ongoing immune-mediated destruction, not nonimmune consumption.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect - a classic board trap. ABO matching should be checked before concluding refractoriness is immune-mediated, but the stem already specifies ABO-identical units were used and immune (HLA) antibodies were already documented, so ABO mismatch does not explain continued failure on HLA-selected platelets.

questions.teaching_point: In immune platelet refractoriness, HLA Class I antibodies are the predominant cause and are managed with HLA-matched/selected (virtual-crossmatch) platelets; if refractoriness persists despite HLA-compatible units, evaluate for platelet-specific (HPA) antibodies (e.g., anti-HPA-1a) and provide HPA antigen-negative platelets. HLA Class II antibodies are a distractor because platelets do not express Class II antigens.

questions.stem:
A 42-year-old man presents with a 1-week history of petechiae, gum bleeding, and easy bruising. He has not received a blood transfusion in the preceding several months and is not receiving heparin. He denies fever, confusion, or decreased urine output. Physical examination shows scattered petechiae but is otherwise unremarkable. Laboratory studies reveal an isolated, markedly low platelet count with a normal hemoglobin, normal white blood cell count, and normal coagulation studies; the peripheral blood smear shows no schistocytes. The clinical picture is most consistent with immune thrombocytopenia (ITP) resulting from antibody-mediated platelet destruction.

Which of the following laboratory studies is described as critical to the diagnostic investigation of this patient's platelet-destructive disorder?

options.text: HLA and HPA antibody assays
ADAMTS13 activity assay with calculation of a PLASMIC score
Donath-Landsteiner test
Anti-HPA-1a maternal alloantibody testing

questions.explanation_correct: ITP is a disorder of antibody-mediated platelet destruction, and the approved curriculum source states that HLA and HPA antibody assays are critical to its investigation. This patient's isolated thrombocytopenia with an otherwise normal blood count, normal coagulation studies, and no schistocytes fits a platelet-destructive process rather than a thrombotic microangiopathy, red cell autoimmune hemolytic process, or a maternal-fetal alloimmune process, making platelet (HLA/HPA) antibody assays the study specifically identified as key to work-up.

option_explanations.A: Correct: the source material explicitly states that ITP involves platelet destruction and that HLA and HPA antibody assays are critical to its investigation, matching this patient's isolated immune-mediated thrombocytopenia.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: ADAMTS13 activity testing and the PLASMIC score are used to work up TTP, which is defined by a pentad of microangiopathic hemolytic anemia, thrombocytopenia, neurologic signs, fever, and renal dysfunction. This patient lacks schistocytes, fever, neurologic changes, and renal dysfunction, arguing against TTP.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: the Donath-Landsteiner test identifies the biphasic IgG anti-P antibody used to diagnose paroxysmal cold hemoglobinuria, a red-cell autoimmune hemolytic process, not a platelet-destructive disorder, so it does not address this patient's isolated thrombocytopenia.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: anti-HPA-1a maternal alloantibody testing is used to investigate fetal and neonatal alloimmune thrombocytopenia, in which maternal antibodies (most commonly anti-HPA-1a) destroy fetal platelets. This is a maternal-fetal alloimmune process and is not applicable to an adult male patient.

questions.teaching_point: ITP is an antibody-mediated platelet-destructive disorder for which HLA and HPA antibody assays are the diagnostic study identified in the curriculum; this must be distinguished from other conditions that share thrombocytopenia or platelet-antigen serology (TTP, PCH, FNAIT) but have distinct pathophysiology, clinical pentads, and target populations.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman with a well-documented history of heparin-induced thrombocytopenia (HIT) is admitted with microangiopathic hemolytic anemia, severe thrombocytopenia, neurologic changes, fever, and renal dysfunction. Thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura (TTP) is diagnosed, and urgent therapeutic plasma exchange (TPE) is ordered. Which anticoagulant is most appropriate for the extracorporeal circuit during this patient's TPE procedure?

options.text: ACD-A (citrate)
Unfractionated heparin
5% human serum albumin
Fresh frozen plasma (FFP)

questions.explanation_correct: ACD-A (citrate) is the anticoagulant described as the primary/standard choice for apheresis procedures, while heparin anticoagulation is reserved for select circumstances such as lipoprotein apheresis rather than routine use. Because this patient's HIT resulted from heparin exposure, re-exposing her to heparin for extracorporeal circuit anticoagulation during TPE is inappropriate; the standard citrate-based anticoagulation should be used instead.

option_explanations.A: Correct: ACD-A (citrate) is the primary/standard anticoagulant used for apheresis procedures, and using it avoids re-exposing a patient with a HIT history to heparin.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: heparin anticoagulation is reserved for select apheresis procedures such as lipoprotein apheresis rather than being the default agent, and re-administering heparin is inappropriate given this patient's documented HIT.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: 5% human serum albumin is described as the standard replacement fluid for most TPE procedures, not an anticoagulant used to prevent clotting in the extracorporeal circuit.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: plasma (FFP) is the replacement fluid of choice specifically for TTP because it supplies ADAMTS13, but it is not used to anticoagulate the apheresis circuit.

questions.teaching_point: Anticoagulant choice for the apheresis extracorporeal circuit (citrate/ACD-A vs. heparin) is a separate decision from replacement fluid choice (albumin vs. plasma); a documented HIT history should prompt avoidance of heparin-based anticoagulation during apheresis.

source_references.supports: Confirms the TTP pentad (microangiopathic hemolytic anemia, thrombocytopenia, neurologic signs, fever, renal dysfunction) used to build the stem, and that TPE removes the ADAMTS13 inhibitor.
States ACD-A (citrate) is the primary anticoagulant for apheresis while heparin is used only for specific procedures like lipoprotein apheresis - basis for choosing citrate over heparin.

States 5% albumin is the standard replacement fluid for most TPE and plasma (FFP) is the replacement fluid of choice for TTP - basis for the albumin and FFP distractors being wrong category (replacement fluid, not anticoagulant).
Confirms HIT is a named blueprint entity grouped with ITP under classification/pathogenesis, but provides no further HIT-specific pathophysiology, diagnostic, or management detail.

review.notes:
Verified against all four sources directly.

questions.stem: A hospitalized patient receiving heparin therapy develops a significant drop in platelet count. During board review, the attending notes that this condition — Heparin-Induced Thrombocytopenia (HIT) — is classified together with Immune Thrombocytopenic Purpura (ITP) under the same "Immune Thrombocytopenia" subdomain of clinical practice, rather than being grouped with nonimmune causes of low platelet counts. Based on this classification, which of the following best describes the general pathogenic category to which HIT belongs?

options.text: An antibody-mediated (immune) process resulting in platelet destruction/activation, analogous in category to ITP
Nonimmunologic protein adsorption (NIPA), in which the drug modifies the cell membrane so plasma proteins coat it nonspecifically without a true antibody response
Maternal alloantibody formation against a fetal platelet-specific antigen such as HPA-1a
A direct, nonimmune toxic effect of the drug on megakaryocytes, independent of any antibody response

questions.explanation_correct: The board curriculum source explicitly lists "classification and pathogenesis of Immune Thrombocytopenia (ITP) and Heparin-Induced Thrombocytopenia (HIT)" as a single shared major concept within the "Immune Thrombocytopenia" subdomain of the Clinical Practice domain. Grouping HIT with ITP under this heading indicates that HIT is understood as an antibody/immune-mediated disorder of platelet destruction or activation, not a nonimmune drug effect. The approved sources do not provide further mechanistic detail (e.g., specific target antigen, antibody isotype, or receptor-mediated platelet activation) beyond this classification, so the item is limited to the categorical distinction the sources support.

option_explanations.A: Correct: the curriculum source groups HIT with ITP under "Immune Thrombocytopenia," indicating an antibody/immune-mediated mechanism rather than a nonimmune one.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: NIPA is a mechanism described in the sources specifically for drug-induced immune hemolytic anemia of red cells, where the drug alters the membrane so proteins adsorb nonspecifically without true antibody specificity — this is a distinct, non-antibody-driven process and is not how the sources classify HIT.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: maternal anti-HPA-1a antibody formation against fetal platelets is the mechanism described in the sources for Fetal and Neonatal Alloimmune Thrombocytopenia (FNAIT), a distinct entity from HIT.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: the sources place HIT within the "Immune Thrombocytopenia" subdomain alongside ITP, not among nonimmune, directly myelosuppressive drug reactions.

questions.teaching_point: HIT is categorized as an immune (antibody-mediated) thrombocytopenic disorder, grouped with ITP, rather than a nonimmune drug effect — a classification-level distinction that must be separated from mechanistically similar but distinct entities such as NIPA (nonimmune) and FNAIT (alloantibody against fetal platelet antigen).

questions.stem: A healthy 24-year-old woman in her first pregnancy delivers a term neonate. At several hours of life, the neonate is noted to have widespread petechiae and scattered ecchymoses. A complete blood count shows an isolated, severe thrombocytopenia with a normal hemoglobin and normal white blood cell count. Cranial ultrasound reveals a small intraparenchymal hemorrhage. The mother's platelet count and coagulation studies are normal, and her antenatal red cell antibody screen was negative throughout pregnancy. Which of the following is the most likely mechanism responsible for this neonate's presentation?

options.text: Maternal IgG anti-HPA-1a crosses the placenta and destroys fetal platelets bearing the paternally inherited HPA-1a antigen
Maternal IgG anti-D crosses the placenta and causes extravascular destruction of fetal red cells
Naturally occurring maternal IgG anti-A or anti-B crosses the placenta and causes hemolysis of fetal red cells
Maternal IgG anti-HNA-1a crosses the placenta and destroys fetal neutrophils
Maternal IgG anti-K suppresses fetal erythroid precursors and impairs erythropoiesis

questions.explanation_correct: Fetal and neonatal alloimmune thrombocytopenia (FNAIT) is caused most commonly by maternal IgG anti-HPA-1a, which crosses the placenta (via active IgG transport, principally IgG1/IgG3) and destroys fetal platelets bearing the paternally inherited HPA-1a antigen that the mother lacks. Unlike Rh HDFN, which typically requires a prior sensitizing pregnancy or exposure, FNAIT can affect a first pregnancy. It classically presents with petechiae, ecchymoses, and life-threatening intracranial hemorrhage from isolated platelet destruction — matching this neonate's isolated severe thrombocytopenia, intraparenchymal hemorrhage, and otherwise normal hemoglobin/white count.

option_explanations.A: Correct: anti-HPA-1a is the antibody most commonly implicated in FNAIT, which selectively destroys fetal platelets and classically presents with petechiae, ecchymoses, and intracranial hemorrhage — and, unlike HDFN, can occur in a first pregnancy.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: anti-D causes extravascular destruction of fetal red cells (Rh HDFN), producing anemia rather than isolated thrombocytopenia; Rh HDFN also typically requires prior maternal sensitization and does not usually occur in a first pregnancy, unlike this scenario.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: ABO HDFN also targets fetal red cells (hemolysis, jaundice) rather than platelets. Although naturally occurring anti-A/anti-B can affect a first pregnancy, this mechanism does not explain isolated thrombocytopenia and intracranial hemorrhage with a normal hemoglobin.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: anti-HNA-1a IgG mediates neonatal alloimmune neutropenia by destroying fetal neutrophils, which would produce neutropenia and infection risk, not thrombocytopenia; this neonate's white count is normal.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect: anti-K suppresses fetal erythroid precursors, producing anemia through impaired erythropoiesis rather than hemolysis or thrombocytopenia; this neonate's hemoglobin is normal, arguing against an erythroid-suppressive mechanism.

questions.teaching_point: FNAIT, most commonly due to maternal anti-HPA-1a IgG, destroys fetal platelets and can occur in a first pregnancy (unlike Rh HDFN), presenting with petechiae, ecchymoses, and risk of intracranial hemorrhage — distinct from RBC-directed alloimmune mechanisms (Rh/ABO HDFN, anti-K erythroid suppression) and neutrophil-directed neonatal alloimmune neutropenia (anti-HNA-1a).

source_references.supports: FNAIT: maternal antibodies target fetal platelets, most commonly anti-HPA-1a, often presenting as ICH in the first pregnancy
Board trap: ABO HDFN can occur in a first pregnancy; Rh HDFN typically does not unless the mother was previously sensitized
Anti-K targets Kell antigens on RBC precursors, causing suppression of erythropoiesis rather than hemolysis
FNAIT presents with petechiae, ecchymoses, or life-threatening intracranial hemorrhage and, unlike HDFN, can affect the first pregnancy; anti-HPA-1a is the most common antibody
Only IgG antibodies (IgG1/IgG3) are actively transported across the placenta, making them the primary cause of both HDFN and FNAIT
Platelets carry Human Platelet Antigens (HPA), which are central to the investigation of FNAIT

Neonatal alloimmune neutropenia (NAN) results from maternal IgG antibodies to paternal HNA (most commonly anti-HNA-1a) crossing the placenta and destroying fetal neutrophils, a distinct cell lineage from FNAIT

questions.stem:
A 45-year-old man with acute myeloid leukemia (AML) undergoing induction chemotherapy has received multiple ABO-identical, leukoreduced apheresis platelet transfusions over the past three weeks. Today his pretransfusion platelet count is 6,000/µL. He receives one apheresis platelet unit, and his 1-hour posttransfusion count is 58,000/µL — a robust, well-above-threshold increment. Over the next 24 hours he becomes febrile with rigors, blood cultures are drawn, and his platelet count falls back to 7,000/µL.

Which of the following best explains this transfusion pattern?

options.text: Nonimmune platelet consumption (e.g., related to his evolving infection); this pattern does not support HLA-antibody-mediated destruction
Immune-mediated refractoriness from antibodies to HLA Class I antigens; HLA-matched platelets should be requested going forward
Immune-mediated refractoriness from antibodies to HLA Class II antigens expressed on the platelet surface
ABO mismatch between the donor platelet unit and the recipient is the most likely explanation
Alloimmunization to a human platelet antigen (e.g., anti-HPA-1a); HPA-antigen-negative platelets should be selected next

questions.explanation_correct: A good 1-hour increment followed by a decline back to near-baseline by 24 hours is the classic pattern of nonimmune platelet consumption rather than immune (HLA-antibody) destruction. Immune-mediated refractoriness is defined by a poor 1-hour CCI; when the 1-hour count is adequate but the 24-hour count has decayed, the source material specifically attributes this to nonimmune consumption (e.g., sepsis/DIC, splenomegaly), which fits this patient's new fever and rigors with pending cultures. This timing distinction (1-hour for immune destruction vs. 24-hour for survival/nonimmune consumption) is explicitly flagged as a high-yield distinguishing point and board trap.

option_explanations.A: Correct: adequate 1-hour increment with poor 24-hour survival is the described pattern for nonimmune consumption (e.g., sepsis/DIC), not HLA-antibody-mediated destruction, which instead produces a poor 1-hour increment.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: HLA Class I antibody-mediated (immune) refractoriness classically produces a poor 1-hour increment/CCI on transfusion, whereas this patient had a robust 1-hour response, arguing against immune HLA destruction.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect distractor: platelets do not express HLA Class II antigens (Class II expression is restricted to antigen-presenting cells such as B lymphocytes and macrophages); antibodies to Class II reflect sensitization to contaminating leukocytes and are not a cause of platelet destruction.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: the stem specifies an ABO-identical transfusion, so ABO mismatch (which can lower increments, e.g., in group O recipients given group A platelets) does not apply here.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect: selection of HPA-antigen-negative platelets is reserved for refractoriness that persists despite HLA-compatible/matched units once platelet-specific (HPA) antibodies are identified; this patient's pattern reflects nonimmune consumption, not confirmed immune refractoriness requiring that workup.

questions.teaching_point: Distinguishing immune from nonimmune platelet transfusion refractoriness hinges on the timing of posttransfusion counts: a poor 1-hour increment/CCI points to immune (usually HLA Class I antibody) destruction, whereas an adequate 1-hour increment followed by decay to baseline by 24 hours points to nonimmune consumption (sepsis, DIC, splenomegaly, fever). Platelets lack HLA Class II antigens, and ABO mismatch and HPA alloimmunization should be considered/excluded as alternative explanations before committing to an HLA-matched or HPA-selected platelet strategy.

source_references.locator: Diagnostic Approach / Common Board Traps and Distractors sections
source_references.supports: 1-hour count timing assesses immune-mediated destruction while 18-24 hour timing assesses platelet survival; a good 1-hour count with poor 24-hour result indicates nonimmune consumption (e.g., sepsis/DIC), not HLA antibodies.

source_references.locator: Common Board Traps and Distractors - HLA Class II Distractor

questions.stem: A 52-year-old woman with acute myeloid leukemia has poor platelet count increments despite receiving ABO-identical, fresh, single-donor apheresis platelets during induction chemotherapy. HLA antibody testing confirms alloimmunization, and the transfusion service arranges HLA-selected platelet donors matched at the MHC loci whose gene products are expressed on the platelet surface itself. Which of the following best describes the class of MHC molecules responsible for the antigens targeted in this patient's alloimmune platelet refractoriness?

options.text: HLA class I (HLA-A, -B, -C), expressed on most nucleated cells and platelets, presenting antigen to CD8+ T cells
HLA class II (HLA-DR, -DQ, -DP), expressed on most nucleated cells and platelets, presenting antigen to CD4+ T cells
MHC class III, encoding complement components such as C2 and C4, expressed on platelets and presenting antigen to CD8+ T cells
HLA class I, restricted to antigen-presenting cells such as B lymphocytes and macrophages, presenting antigen to CD4+ T cells
HLA class II (HLA-DR, -DQ, -DP), restricted to antigen-presenting cells, presenting antigen to CD8+ T cells

questions.explanation_correct: The MHC Class I region (HLA-A, -B, -C loci) is expressed on most nucleated cells and on platelets, which is why platelets carry HLA Class I antigens and become targets of anti-HLA alloantibodies causing refractoriness; HLA-selected platelet donors are chosen to match these Class I specificities. Class I molecules present antigen to CD8+ T cells, distinguishing them from the APC-restricted, CD4+-presenting Class II molecules.

option_explanations.A: Correct. Class I loci (HLA-A, -B, -C) are expressed on most nucleated cells and platelets and present antigen to CD8+ T cells, matching the source-defined MHC Class I organization and function.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. This swaps Class II's true tissue distribution: Class II (HLA-DR, -DQ, -DP) is restricted to antigen-presenting cells such as B lymphocytes and macrophages, not broadly expressed on nucleated cells and platelets, so it is not the class responsible for platelet-surface antigens.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. MHC Class III genes encode complement components (C2, C4) and cytokines rather than classic histocompatibility antigens; Class III is not expressed as a surface antigen-presenting molecule on platelets and does not present peptide antigen to T cells.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. This correctly names Class I but incorrectly assigns it Class II's APC-restricted distribution and Class II's CD4+ T-cell restriction; Class I is broadly distributed on nucleated cells/platelets and presents to CD8+ T cells.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect. This correctly restricts Class II to antigen-presenting cells but incorrectly assigns it CD8+ T-cell restriction; Class II presents antigen to CD4+ T cells, not CD8+.

questions.teaching_point: MHC Class I (HLA-A, -B, -C) is broadly expressed on nearly all nucleated cells and platelets and presents antigen to CD8+ T cells; Class II (HLA-DR, -DQ, -DP) is restricted to professional antigen-presenting cells and presents to CD4+ T cells; Class III encodes complement (C2, C4) and cytokine genes rather than classic histocompatibility molecules. Confusing these distributions/T-cell restrictions is a common board trap, and it is Class I antigens on platelets that drive alloimmune platelet refractoriness requiring HLA-selected platelets.

questions.subdomain: Class I and II Antigens and Their Function
questions.stem: A 54-year-old woman with acute myeloid leukemia undergoing induction chemotherapy has received multiple ABO-compatible, fresh apheresis platelet transfusions. On two consecutive transfusions, her 1-hour posttransfusion corrected count increment (CCI) is less than 5,000/µL. HLA antibody testing by a multiantigen-bead solid-phase assay detects antibodies reactive with HLA-DR and HLA-DQ specificities; no reactivity is detected against HLA-A or HLA-B specificities. Which of the following is the most accurate interpretation of these antibody findings in relation to her platelet refractoriness?

options.text: The detected antibodies are the cause of her refractoriness, since activated platelets upregulate HLA Class II antigens that become antibody targets.

The detected antibodies most likely reflect immunization to HLA Class II antigens carried by contaminating leukocytes in prior blood products; because platelets do not express HLA Class II antigens, this pattern does not explain the immune-mediated platelet destruction.
Providing platelets that are zero-mismatched for HLA-A and -B will resolve her refractoriness, since HLA Class I matching addresses the antibodies identified in this workup.
Because HLA Class I antigens are restricted to professional antigen-presenting cells, the absence of anti-HLA-A/B reactivity effectively excludes an immune basis for her refractoriness.
These results confirm alloimmunization to human platelet antigens (HPA), and HPA-antigen-negative platelet units should now be selected.

questions.explanation_correct: Platelets carry ABO antigens, HLA Class I (HLA-A and -B), and human platelet antigens (HPA), but they do NOT express HLA Class II antigens (HLA-DR, -DQ, -DP) — this is an explicitly flagged board trap. When a refractory patient's antibody screen shows reactivity limited to Class II specificities, the correct interpretation is that the patient has been sensitized to Class II antigens on contaminating leukocytes present in prior cellular blood products, not to an antigen actually carried on the transfused platelets. Since platelets lack the target antigen, anti-Class II antibodies cannot mediate immune destruction of the platelets, and this finding does not account for her poor CCI. The immune target that actually matters for HLA-mediated platelet refractoriness is HLA Class I (A and B), consistent with the broader teaching that Class I antigens are expressed on nearly all nucleated cells (including platelets) while Class II antigens are restricted to antigen-presenting cells.

option_explanations.A: Incorrect. The sources state platelets do NOT express HLA Class II antigens; there is no basis in the material for activation-induced Class II expression on platelets, so anti-DR/DQ antibodies cannot mediate platelet destruction by this mechanism.
option_explanations.B: Correct. Anti-HLA Class II reactivity in a refractory patient is attributed to leukocyte sensitization from contaminating WBCs in prior products; because platelets lack HLA Class II, this antibody pattern does not explain immune platelet destruction, and a Class I or HPA antibody (or a nonimmune cause) should still be sought.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect reasoning. HLA-A/B (Class I) zero-mismatch matching is the strategy used to avoid destruction mediated by Class I antibodies, but the antibodies identified in this patient are Class II (DR/DQ), not Class I — so this matching strategy does not logically address the antibodies actually detected.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect and reverses the biology: HLA Class I antigens (HLA-A, -B, -C) are found on nearly all nucleated cells, while it is HLA Class II antigens (HLA-DR, -DQ, -DP) that are restricted to antigen-presenting cells. The absence of anti-A/B reactivity also does not by itself exclude an immune contribution to refractoriness.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect. The workup described identified HLA (Class II) antibodies, not platelet-specific (HPA) antibodies. HPA-antigen-negative selection is indicated when refractoriness persists despite HLA-compatible units and platelet-specific antibodies such as anti-HPA-1a are identified — neither condition is established by this vignette.

questions.teaching_point: HLA Class I antigens (HLA-A, -B, -C) are expressed on nearly all nucleated cells, including platelets, and are the primary immune target in HLA-mediated platelet refractoriness. HLA Class II antigens (HLA-DR, -DQ, -DP) are restricted to antigen-presenting cells and are NOT expressed on platelets, so anti-Class II antibodies detected during a refractoriness workup reflect leukocyte sensitization from prior blood products, not a mechanism of platelet destruction — a frequently tested board trap.

source_references.supports: MHC Class I (HLA-A, -B, -C) antigens are found on nearly all nucleated cells, while Class II (HLA-DR, -DQ, -DP) antigens are restricted to antigen-presenting cells.

Platelets carry ABO antigens, HLA Class I (A and B), and human platelet antigens (HPA) as their relevant antigen systems.

questions.stem: A former 27-week-gestation preterm infant, now 8 weeks old, becomes progressively pale with worsening tachycardia, tachypnea, poor weight gain, and an increasing supplemental oxygen requirement over two weeks despite adequate nutritional intake and no evidence of acute blood loss. The infant's initial newborn ABO/Rh type and antibody screen were compatible with the mother's, and the direct antiglobulin test (DAT) has remained negative throughout the admission. Which of the following best explains the primary pathophysiologic mechanism responsible for this infant's anemia?

options.text: A blunted erythropoietin response to the falling hemoglobin, a recognized feature of neonatal red cell physiology
Ongoing extravascular hemolysis from unresolved maternal IgG alloantibodies against fetal RBC antigens (HDFN)
Progressive depletion of 2,3-DPG and ATP in the infant's circulating red cells from prolonged in vitro storage
Impaired anaerobic glycolysis through the Embden-Meyerhof pathway reducing ATP generation in the infant's red cells
Ongoing fetomaternal hemorrhage requiring Kleihauer-Betke quantification to guide RhIG dosing

questions.explanation_correct: Neonates younger than 4 months have a blunted erythropoietin response to anemia, a core feature of neonatal red cell physiology. This inadequate EPO output for the degree of falling hemoglobin is the mechanism underlying the clinical picture described (tachycardia, tachypnea, poor weight gain, increasing oxygen requirement) in a preterm infant with no evidence of hemolysis (negative DAT) or active bleeding — matching the anemia-of-prematurity presentation the sources describe alongside blunted neonatal EPO physiology.

option_explanations.A: Correct: neonates have an immature, blunted erythropoietin response to anemia, and this infant's presentation (tachycardia, tachypnea, poor weight gain, rising oxygen need) matches the described anemia-of-prematurity picture with no alternative cause identified.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: HDFN from maternal IgG alloantibodies typically produces a positive DAT with anti-IgG; this infant's DAT has remained negative throughout admission, arguing against ongoing immune-mediated hemolysis.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: 2,3-DPG/ATP depletion is the storage lesion that develops in banked RBC units stored in vitro outside the body, not a process that occurs in an infant's own circulating red cells.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: while mature RBCs do rely on anaerobic (Embden-Meyerhof) glycolysis for the majority of their ATP, this is a general feature of all mature red cells, not a described mechanism of new-onset anemia in this scenario, and nothing suggests a metabolic defect.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect: fetomaternal hemorrhage screening (Rosette/Kleihauer-Betke) and RhIG dosing are maternal, peripartum interventions used around the time of delivery, not a mechanism of progressive anemia developing in an infant weeks after birth.

source_references.supports: Neonates (<4 months) have immature organ systems, small blood volumes, and a blunted EPO response to anemia; newborn DAT with anti-IgG is typically positive in HDFN; Rosette/Kleihauer-Betke testing and RhIG dosing occur around delivery.

States the same blunted neonatal EPO response to anemia, and separately describes anemia of prematurity presenting with tachycardia, tachypnea, poor weight gain, or increased oxygen requirement; confirms DAT-positive pattern in HDFN and FMH/KB testing tied to RhIG dosing.

Lists 'erythropoietin regulation' as a major concept within the RBCs and RBC Components domain, confirming blueprint relevance of the topic.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman has had a stable hemoglobin of 8.5 g/dL for several months due to chronic anemia. Over this period her body has adapted physiologically: her resting cardiac output has increased, her red cell 2,3-DPG concentration has risen (shifting the hemoglobin-oxygen dissociation curve to the right), and blood flow has been redistributed toward vital organs to preserve tissue oxygen delivery. In addition to these adaptations, her bone marrow has begun producing new red cells at an increased rate to replace those being lost. Which of the following is primarily responsible for driving this increase in red cell production?

options.text: Erythropoietin
Increased red cell 2,3-DPG concentration
Increased cardiac output
Redistribution of blood flow to vital organs
A rightward shift of the hemoglobin-oxygen dissociation curve

questions.explanation_correct: The approved sources state directly that red cell production is regulated by erythropoietin. The other physiologic changes described in the vignette (increased cardiac output, altered 2,3-DPG/oxygen affinity, redistribution of blood flow) are compensatory mechanisms that improve oxygen delivery from the existing red cell mass; they do not stimulate the marrow to produce new red cells. Only erythropoietin drives the increase in red cell production itself.

option_explanations.A: Correct. The sources state that red cell production is regulated by erythropoietin, distinguishing production from mechanisms that merely improve oxygen delivery or affinity.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect distractor. Increased 2,3-DPG is a documented compensatory adaptation to anemia that shifts the oxygen dissociation curve and decreases hemoglobin's oxygen affinity so more oxygen is released to tissues, but it does not increase marrow production of new red cells.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect distractor. Increased cardiac output is listed among the body's compensatory mechanisms to anemia, improving delivery of existing red cells to tissues, but it is not the mechanism that stimulates red cell production.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect distractor. Redistribution of blood flow to vital organs is a compensatory mechanism to anemia that preserves oxygen delivery to critical organs, but it does not increase red cell production.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect distractor. A rightward shift of the oxygen dissociation curve (from increased 2,3-DPG, temperature, or decreased pH) facilitates oxygen release to tissues, but it reflects a change in hemoglobin's oxygen affinity for gas transport, not stimulation of new red cell production.

questions.teaching_point: Erythropoietin is the specific regulator that drives increased red cell production; other physiologic adaptations to anemia (cardiac output, 2,3-DPG/oxygen affinity, blood flow redistribution) improve oxygen delivery from the existing red cell mass but do not themselves increase marrow output of new red cells.

questions.stem: A blood bank technologist reviewing a quality-control log notes that a unit of packed RBCs stored in CPDA-1 anticoagulant for 30 days shows a marked decrease in supernatant glucose concentration and a corresponding rise in lactic acid compared with values recorded at collection. The unit is visually normal in color, with no discoloration to suggest contamination. Which of the following best explains this biochemical pattern?

options.text: Mature erythrocytes lack mitochondria and rely on anaerobic glycolysis (Embden-Meyerhof pathway) for essentially all ATP production, a process that consumes glucose and generates lactate as its end product
Erythrocytes normally generate ATP through mitochondrial oxidative phosphorylation, and impaired mitochondrial function during storage shunts glucose toward lactate production
Most of the glucose consumed during storage is diverted through the Luebering-Rapoport shunt to generate 2,3-DPG, which is subsequently converted into lactic acid
Bacterial proliferation within the unit is anaerobically fermenting glucose to lactic acid as a byproduct of contamination
Progressive potassium efflux from the cell activates membrane-bound glycolytic enzymes, driving glucose turnover and lactate formation independent of ATP demand

questions.explanation_correct: Mature RBCs are anucleate and lack mitochondria, so they depend on anaerobic glycolysis via the Embden-Meyerhof pathway for about 90% of their ATP. This pathway consumes glucose and yields lactate (lactic acid) as its terminal metabolic product. Over storage, ongoing glycolysis in the absence of any oxidative pathway produces exactly the pattern described in the log: falling glucose, falling pH, and rising lactic acid — the expected biochemical signature of the storage lesion, not a sign of pathology.

option_explanations.A: Correct: absence of mitochondria forces reliance on anaerobic glycolysis, which consumes glucose and produces lactate — directly accounting for both the falling glucose and rising lactic acid seen on storage.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: mature RBCs have no mitochondria at all, so oxidative phosphorylation never occurs in these cells; the glucose-to-lactate pattern reflects the cell's normal (not impaired) anaerobic glycolytic physiology, present from the moment of collection, not a storage-induced dysfunction.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: the Luebering-Rapoport shunt is a minor branch off glycolysis that generates 2,3-DPG to regulate hemoglobin oxygen affinity; 2,3-DPG itself is depleted (not converted into lactate) during storage, and this shunt is not the main route by which glucose is consumed to produce lactate.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: bacterial contamination classically presents with a murky, brown, or purple-discolored unit requiring quarantine; the unit described here is visually normal, so the glucose/lactate pattern reflects expected anaerobic glycolysis rather than infection.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect: potassium efflux from stored RBCs is a real storage-lesion finding, but it results from failure of the ATP-dependent membrane pump as ATP declines — it is not a mechanism that drives glucose consumption or lactate formation.

questions.teaching_point: Mature RBCs lack mitochondria and depend almost entirely on anaerobic glycolysis (Embden-Meyerhof pathway) for ATP; this is the mechanistic basis for the classic storage-lesion pattern of falling glucose/pH and rising lactic acid/potassium.

questions.stem: A blood bank medical director reviews quality-control data on a unit of red blood cells stored in CPDA-1 that is nearing the end of its permitted shelf life. A peripheral smear prepared from the unit shows a shift from normal biconcave discs to spiculated, contracted echinocytes with shedding of small membrane-derived microvesicles, and osmotic fragility testing on the unit shows increased fragility compared with a freshly collected unit. Which of the following best explains the underlying mechanism of these membrane changes?

options.text: Progressive depletion of ATP from declining anaerobic (Embden-Meyerhof) glycolysis during storage, impairing maintenance of the red cell's normal membrane integrity
Depletion of 2,3-DPG generated by the Luebering-Rapoport shunt, shifting the oxyhemoglobin dissociation curve to the left
Leaching of the DEHP plasticizer out of the PVC storage bag, which destabilizes the red cell membrane
Rising extracellular potassium concentration in the storage supernatant, which is the direct cause of the shape change
Accumulating lactic acid from ongoing glycolysis lowering pH enough to directly denature the membrane cytoskeleton

questions.explanation_correct: Mature RBCs lack mitochondria and rely on anaerobic (Embden-Meyerhof) glycolysis for approximately 90% of ATP production. During in-vitro storage, ATP progressively declines as part of the storage lesion, and the sources describe an explicit "ATP/2,3-DPG divergence": ATP maintenance corresponds to red cell viability/membrane integrity, while 2,3-DPG maintenance corresponds to oxygen-delivery function. The storage lesion definition itself pairs decreased ATP (a biochemical change) with disc-to-echinocyte transformation, microvesicle formation, and increased osmotic fragility (morphologic changes), supporting ATP depletion as the mechanistic link to the membrane changes described in this vignette.

option_explanations.A: Correct. Mature RBCs lack mitochondria and depend on anaerobic glycolysis for about 90% of ATP; ATP decline is explicitly tied to maintenance of red cell 'viability' as distinct from 2,3-DPG's role in 'function,' and the storage lesion definition pairs decreased ATP with the morphologic changes of disc-to-echinocyte transformation, microvesicle formation, and increased osmotic fragility.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. 2,3-DPG depletion is described as affecting oxygen-carrying 'function' (left shift of the oxyhemoglobin dissociation curve, decreased tissue oxygen release), not the structural/membrane changes seen on the smear; the sources explicitly separate this from the ATP-related 'viability' changes.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect and reversed. The sources state that DEHP in PVC bags actually stabilizes the RBC membrane and reduces hemolysis during storage, despite toxicity concerns — the opposite of what this option claims.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. Increased plasma/supernatant potassium is listed as a biochemical marker of the storage lesion (reflecting failure of ATP-dependent membrane pump function), but it is not described as the cause of the disc-to-echinocyte transformation.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect. Increased lactic acid and decreased pH are part of the biochemical storage lesion, but the sources do not attribute the morphologic membrane changes to direct acid-mediated denaturation of cytoskeletal proteins; the viability-related change they describe is tied to ATP, not lactic acid.

questions.teaching_point: ATP depletion from declining anaerobic glycolysis during RBC storage impairs membrane/cytoskeletal integrity ("viability"), producing the disc-to-echinocyte transformation, microvesicle shedding, and increased osmotic fragility of the storage lesion — distinct from 2,3-DPG depletion, which impairs oxygen-delivery "function" via a left-shifted oxyhemoglobin dissociation curve. A related board trap: DEHP plasticizer in PVC storage bags, despite toxicity concerns, actually stabilizes the RBC membrane and reduces storage hemolysis.

questions.subdomain: Red Cell Preservation in Transfusion Medicine
questions.stem: A hospitalist caring for a hemodynamically stable, non-bleeding patient with chronic anemia calls the blood bank and asks that only red blood cell (RBC) units stored for less than 10 days be issued. The hospitalist is concerned that the biochemical and morphologic changes that accumulate in RBC units during refrigerated storage ("storage lesion") will worsen the patient's clinical outcome if an older unit is transfused. Before responding, the blood bank physician reviews the storage physiology and the available clinical evidence. Which of the following statements best reflects the current evidence and physiology regarding RBC storage age?

options.text: Multiple randomized controlled trials (e.g., ARIPI, ABLE, TRANSFUSE) have shown no difference in mortality between recipients of fresher versus older-stored RBC units, so routine restriction to "fresh" blood is not supported by the evidence.
Because 2,3-DPG is depleted during storage, older units develop a right-shifted oxygen dissociation curve, causing greater oxygen release to tissues than fresher units.
All RBC units may be stored for up to 42 days before transfusion, regardless of which anticoagulant-preservative solution was used to collect them.
The storage lesion has been proven in randomized trials to directly increase recipient mortality, which justifies preferential use of fresh units in this patient.
Supernatant potassium concentration falls progressively as an RBC unit ages, so hyperkalemia risk is actually lower with older units.

questions.explanation_correct: RBC storage does produce a real "storage lesion" — decreased pH, ATP, and 2,3-DPG with increased supernatant potassium and lactic acid — and 2,3-DPG depletion left-shifts the oxygen dissociation curve, increasing hemoglobin-oxygen affinity and reducing oxygen unloading to tissues. However, despite these measurable biochemical changes, multiple randomized controlled trials (ARIPI, ABLE, TRANSFUSE) specifically compared clinical outcomes between recipients of fresher versus older-stored RBC units and found no difference in mortality. This is a classic board trap: the existence of a storage lesion does not translate into demonstrated clinical harm, so a blanket request for "fresh" blood in a stable, non-bleeding patient is not evidence-based.

option_explanations.A: Correct. The sources explicitly note that despite the storage lesion, RCTs (ARIPI, ABLE, TRANSFUSE) showed no mortality difference between fresh and older RBCs, making routine fresh-blood restriction unsupported.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect and physiologically reversed: 2,3-DPG depletion during storage causes a LEFT shift of the oxygen dissociation curve (increased oxygen affinity), meaning LESS oxygen is released to tissues, not more.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: maximum storage duration depends on the anticoagulant-preservative/additive solution used — CPD/CP2D units are good for 21 days, CPDA-1 for 35 days, and additive solutions such as AS-1/AS-3 extend storage to 42 days. Not all units reach 42 days.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: the cited randomized trials found no mortality difference attributable to storage duration, so a causal link between storage lesion and increased mortality has not been demonstrated, undermining the rationale for preferential fresh-unit use.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect and reversed: storage lesion markers include INCREASED (not decreased) supernatant potassium and lactic acid, along with decreased glucose and pH, as RBC units age.

questions.teaching_point: RBC storage lesion (falling pH/ATP/2,3-DPG, rising K+/lactate, left-shifted oxygen dissociation curve) is real, but landmark RCTs (ARIPI, ABLE, TRANSFUSE) found no mortality difference between fresh and older-stored RBC units, so age-of-blood-based transfusion restrictions are not evidence-based. Maximum storage duration depends on the anticoagulant-preservative system: CPD/CP2D = 21 days, CPDA-1 = 35 days, AS-1/AS-3 additive solutions = 42 days.

source_references.supports: Defines storage lesion (decreased pH/ATP/2,3-DPG, increased plasma K+), notes 2,3-DPG depletion causes a left-shifted O2 curve with less O2 release, lists supernatant markers (decreased glucose/pH, increased lactic acid/K+), and states RCTs (ARIPI, ABLE, TRANSFUSE) showed no mortality difference between fresh and older RBCs, plus anticoagulant-specific storage durations (CPD/CP2D=21d, CPDA-1=35d,

Confirms that anticoagulant-preservative solutions like CPDA-1 allow storage for 35 days while additive solutions (AS-1, AS-3, etc.) extend shelf life to 42 days, supporting that storage duration varies by solution rather than being uniformly 42 days.

questions.stem: At a community blood drive, a donor experiences a vasovagal reaction partway through phlebotomy and the collection is stopped early. The technologist notes that the primary collection bag, prepared with an anticoagulant volume proportioned for a nominal 450-mL whole-blood draw, contains only 340 mL of blood at the end of the procedure. Which of the following is the most appropriate labeling and disposition of this unit for component separation?

options.text: Label the unit "low volume"; the unit may still be used to separate RBCs, but FFP must not be prepared from this collection.
No special labeling is required, because 340 mL is close enough to the nominal 450-mL draw to be processed as a routine, unrestricted collection.
Label the unit "low volume" and proceed to prepare FFP as usual, since plasma volume is unaffected by a reduced whole-blood draw.
Discard the entire unit, since a collection below 405 mL cannot be used to prepare any blood component.

questions.explanation_correct: Whole blood collected into a 450-mL primary bag is anticoagulated for a nominal 450-mL draw. When the actual collection volume falls into the 300–404 mL range, the anticoagulant-to-blood ratio is altered enough that the unit must be flagged with a "low volume" label, and per the source-defined rule, no FFP may be prepared from that collection. The rule does not prohibit all component separation from the unit — it specifically restricts FFP preparation while still permitting other processing (e.g., RBC separation) to proceed, provided the low-volume label is applied. This 340-mL draw falls squarely in the 300–404 mL range, so labeling it "low volume" and withholding it from FFP preparation is the correct disposition.

option_explanations.A: Correct. The source rule states that a whole blood collection of 300–404 mL in a 450-mL bag must be labeled "low volume," and no FFP can be made from it — implying other components such as RBCs may still be separated from the unit.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. A 340-mL draw falls inside the 300–404 mL range that specifically triggers mandatory "low volume" labeling; it is not treated as a routine, unrestricted collection.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. This is the classic board trap the source calls out directly: a low-volume collection in this range is explicitly excluded from FFP preparation, not processed as usual.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. The source restriction is limited to prohibiting FFP preparation from the unit; it does not state that the unit must be discarded or that no component at all can be made from it.

questions.teaching_point: Whole blood collections of 300–404 mL into a 450-mL primary bag must be labeled "low volume" because the anticoagulant-to-blood ratio deviates from the validated nominal ratio; this specifically precludes FFP preparation from that unit, though other component separation (e.g., RBCs) may still proceed with proper labeling.

source_references.supports: States that a whole blood collection of 300–404 mL into a 450-mL bag must be labeled "low volume" and that no FFP can be made from it, which is the sole basis for the stem, correct answer, and all distractor rationales.

review.notes:
Verified against all four sources. Only SRC-032 addresses this topic, in one terse sentence under "Common Board Traps and Distractors" (line 171): "Low-Volume Units: If whole blood collection is 300–404 mL (in a 450-mL bag), it must be labeled 'low volume,' and no FFP can be made from it." No other source (SRC-028, SRC-052, SRC-047) mentions low-volume units, the 300-404 mL threshold, or FFP restriction — confirmed via targeted grep across all three files (zero matches on "low volume," "300," "404," "vasovagal," "QNS," "insufficient volume," etc.).

questions.stem: A hospital transfusion service is preparing to issue a unit of RBCs that has been stored in AS-3 additive solution for 35 days (within its 42-day dating period) for a hemodynamically stable, non-bleeding postoperative patient with a hemoglobin of 7.2 g/dL. Pre-issue quality testing on a sample from the unit shows that ATP is well preserved, but the 2,3-DPG level is markedly decreased compared to a freshly collected unit. The ordering physician asks the blood bank director whether this "older" additive-solution unit is an appropriate choice compared to a fresher unit. Which of the following best reflects the current understanding of the clinical significance of this laboratory finding?

options.text: Preserved ATP supports in vivo red cell viability, and although the additive solution does not prevent 2,3-DPG depletion during storage, randomized trials have shown no significant mortality difference between fresh and older stored RBC units; transfused cells also restore normal 2,3-DPG levels within about 6 to 24 hours after infusion.
The reduced 2,3-DPG level indicates the unit will not meet the required standard of at least 75% 24-hour post-transfusion red cell recovery, so a fresher unit should be selected instead.
Additive solutions such as AS-3 are specifically formulated to preserve 2,3-DPG along with ATP, so this finding is unexpected and should prompt quarantine of the unit for suspected contamination.

This finding confirms that the red cell storage lesion produces clinically significant harm, consistent with randomized trials demonstrating higher mortality in patients transfused with older RBC units.
Depletion of 2,3-DPG causes a rightward shift of the hemoglobin-oxygen dissociation curve (decreased oxygen affinity), so the unit will impair tissue oxygen delivery immediately upon transfusion.

questions.explanation_correct: The sources describe an "ATP/2,3-DPG divergence" specific to additive solutions: AS-1/AS-3/AS-5 extend storage to 42 days and are excellent at maintaining ATP (the viability correlate), but they do not prevent depletion of 2,3-DPG (the functional/oxygen-affinity correlate) by the second week of storage. Despite this storage lesion, multiple randomized trials (e.g., ARIPI, RECESS/ABLE/TRANSFUSE) found no significant difference in mortality between patients transfused with fresh versus older RBC units. In addition, 2,3-DPG is not a fixed deficit — transfused cells regenerate normal 2,3-DPG levels within roughly 6 to 24 hours after infusion. Together these facts support using the 35-day AS-3 unit for a stable, non-bleeding patient rather than reflexively preferring a fresher unit.

option_explanations.A: Correct: integrates the source-grounded ATP/2,3-DPG divergence, the negative mortality trials for fresh vs. older RBCs, and the known in vivo recovery of 2,3-DPG after transfusion.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: the 75%-at-24-hour post-transfusion recovery standard is a viability benchmark tied to ATP-dependent storage system validation, not a real-time readout of 2,3-DPG level; ATP (the viability correlate) is preserved in this unit, so there is no source-based reason to expect failed recovery.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: the sources explicitly state additive solutions do NOT prevent 2,3-DPG depletion even though they maintain ATP well — this is an expected, well-described storage change, not evidence of contamination requiring quarantine.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: this is the classic "age of blood" board trap — despite storage-lesion changes, large randomized trials (ARIPI, RECESS/ABLE/TRANSFUSE) showed no significant mortality difference between fresh and older RBC units.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect: the sources state that 2,3-DPG depletion produces a LEFT shift of the oxygen dissociation curve (increased hemoglobin-oxygen affinity), the opposite of what this option describes, and the trial evidence does not support a clinically significant immediate harm from this change in stable, non-bleeding patients.

questions.teaching_point: Additive solutions (AS-1, AS-3, AS-5) extend RBC storage to 42 days by preserving ATP-dependent cell viability, but they do not prevent 2,3-DPG depletion (a storage-lesion change affecting oxygen-release function) by the second week of storage. This ATP/2,3-DPG divergence is a classic board distinction: viability (ATP, in vivo survival) is maintained even as function (2,3-DPG, oxygen affinity) declines, yet randomized trials show no mortality difference between fresh and older RBC units, and 2,3-DPG regenerates within hours after transfusion.

source_references.supports: Additive solutions (AS-1/3/5) extend storage to 42 days, maintain ATP well but do not prevent 2,3-DPG depletion by the second storage week; 2,3-DPG recovers in vivo in 6-24h; ARIPI/RECESS trials show no mortality difference fresh vs. older RBCs; low 2,3-DPG causes a left shift (increased O2 affinity).

Storage lesion definition (decreased ATP/2,3-DPG/pH, increased K+); left shift occurs when 2,3-DPG is depleted; ARIPI/ABLE/TRANSFUSE trials show no mortality difference between fresh and older RBC units; restrictive transfusion threshold ~7-8 g/dL.
Confirms additive solutions such as AS-1/AS-3 extend RBC shelf life to 42 days compared with CPDA-1's 35 days.

review.notes: All material claims are grounded, most at near-verbatim level, in SRC-005's "ATP/2,3-DPG Divergence" pearl and "2,3-DPG Recovery" management note, corroborated by SRC-024's parallel storage-lesion/left-shift/trial statements and SRC-010's AS-3 42-day confirmation. questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman receives 2 units of RBCs. Her pretransfusion antibody screen, performed shortly before transfusion, was negative, and the transfusion itself was uneventful. Ten days later she returns with new fatigue, mild scleral icterus, and low-grade fever, with an unexplained drop in hemoglobin from her post-transfusion baseline. A repeat antibody screen is now positive, the DAT shows a mixed-field pattern, and an eluate prepared from her RBCs reacts specifically with reagent cells expressing the Jkb antigen. Which immune mechanism best explains why this antibody was undetectable on the original pretransfusion screen but is now present and causing hemolysis?

options.text: An anamnestic (secondary) immune response: prior sensitization to the Jkb antigen generated memory B lymphocytes that rapidly proliferated and produced antibody upon re-exposure, after the original antibody titer had fallen below the detection threshold of the screen
A primary immune response to an antigen the patient had never previously encountered
Nonimmunologic protein adsorption causing nonspecific coating of the RBC membrane by plasma proteins
IgM-mediated complement fixation from ABO incompatibility, producing acute intravascular hemolysis
Drug-dependent antibody formation that requires continued presence of a sensitizing medication in the test system

questions.explanation_correct: This clinical picture (hemolysis 2-14 days post-transfusion, positive antibody screen and mixed-field DAT on a new sample, eluate identifying a specific alloantibody) is the classic pattern of a delayed hemolytic transfusion reaction (DHTR), which the sources describe as an anamnestic immune response. In DHTR, the patient was previously sensitized (often from prior transfusion or pregnancy) to an RBC antigen such as Kidd (Jk) or Rh, but the resulting antibody titer waned over time and fell below the detection limit of the pretransfusion antibody screen. Re-exposure to the antigen on the transfused donor cells triggers rapid reactivation of immunologic memory, producing a fast, robust secondary antibody response that coats and destroys the transfused cells days after transfusion. The sources specifically flag Kidd antibodies as the classic board answer for this mechanism because they notoriously disappear from the serum between sensitizing events.

option_explanations.A: Correct. This matches the source definition of DHTR as an anamnestic immune response involving antibodies (classically Kidd or Rh) that were below detection limits at the time of the prior screen, with re-exposure driving a rapid secondary (memory) antibody response and delayed hemolysis 2-14 days post-transfusion.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. A primary immune response describes the body's first encounter with a novel antigen, which is typically slower to generate antibody and does not explain the disappearance/reappearance pattern seen here. The clinical picture described in the sources for this scenario is explicitly labeled anamnestic (secondary), not primary.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. Nonimmunologic protein adsorption (NIPA) produces a DAT positive for multiple proteins with a negative serum and negative eluate, meaning there is no true antibody specificity to identify. This case has a positive eluate reacting specifically with Jkb-positive cells, which is inconsistent with NIPA.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. This describes the pathophysiology of an acute hemolytic transfusion reaction (AHTR), which is most commonly due to ABO incompatibility from clerical/identification error and causes intravascular hemolysis within 24 hours of transfusion, not a delayed presentation at day 10 with a mixed-field DAT and a Kidd-specific eluate.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect. Drug-dependent antibodies require the offending drug to be present in the test system (coating the RBC or in solution) to react, and there is no drug exposure described in this vignette. The eluate here reacts with untreated Jkb-positive reagent cells, not drug-treated cells.

questions.teaching_point: Delayed hemolytic transfusion reactions represent an anamnestic (secondary) immune response: prior sensitization creates memory B cells whose antibody titer falls below assay detection, and antigen re-exposure triggers rapid antibody re-production and hemolysis, classically implicating Kidd system antibodies.

questions.stem: During pretransfusion antibody identification, a technologist observes 2+ agglutination at the antihuman globulin (IAT) phase with the patient's serum against several reagent panel red cells. To help resolve the specificity, the same panel red cells are treated with ficin and retested. On the ficin-treated panel, reactions with cells positive for the D (Rh), Jka (Kidd), and P1 antigens become noticeably stronger, but the reaction previously seen with a panel cell positive for the Duffy antigen Fya is completely lost. Which of the following antibody specificities in the patient's serum best accounts for the reaction that disappeared after ficin treatment?

options.text: Anti-Fya
Anti-Jka
Anti-D
Anti-P1

questions.explanation_correct: Proteolytic enzyme treatment of reagent red cells with ficin (or papain) destroys antigens of the Duffy system, so an antibody directed against Fya loses reactivity with ficin-treated Fya-positive cells even though it agglutinated those same cells before treatment. In contrast, ficin/papain treatment enhances reactivity of antibodies to Rh (e.g., D), Kidd (Jka/Jkb), and P1 antigens, which matches the strengthened reactions described in the stem. This selective loss-versus-enhancement pattern on an enzyme panel is a classic technique used during antibody identification to help distinguish Duffy (and MNS) specificities, which disappear, from Rh, Kidd, and P1 specificities, which strengthen.

option_explanations.A: Correct. Ficin/papain enzyme treatment destroys Duffy system antigens, so anti-Fya reactivity is abolished against ficin-treated Fya-positive cells even though it was detectable on the untreated panel, exactly matching the described loss of reactivity.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. Kidd antigens (Jka/Jkb) are enhanced, not destroyed, by ficin/papain treatment, so an anti-Jka would be expected to react more strongly with enzyme-treated cells rather than lose reactivity.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. Rh antigens, including D, are enhanced by ficin/papain treatment, so an anti-D would show stronger reactivity with enzyme-treated cells, not a disappearance of the reaction.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. P1 antigen reactivity is enhanced, not destroyed, by ficin/papain treatment, so an anti-P1 would be expected to react more strongly with enzyme-treated cells rather than show absent reactivity.

questions.teaching_point: Proteolytic enzyme (ficin/papain) treatment of red cells is a key technique in antibody identification: it destroys Duffy and MNS system antigens (causing loss of reactivity for antibodies to those systems) while enhancing Rh, Kidd, and P1 antigen reactivity, helping resolve mixtures of antibodies or confirm specificity.

source_references.supports: States that ficin/papain enzyme treatment destroys MNS and Duffy system antigens while enhancing Rh, Kidd, and P1 antigens; this is the core fact used to construct and resolve the antigen-antibody interaction scenario.

review.notes: Core teaching fact ('Ficin/Papain destroys MNS and Duffy antigens; enhances Rh, Kidd, and P1 antigens') is stated verbatim in SRC-043 (master-board-review-guide-transfusion-medicine.md, line 45) and is the sole basis needed for the stem, key, and all four option rationales. No other source addresses enzyme treatment/antigen susceptibility, and the question's source_references correctly cite only SRC-043 — no fabricated or unsupported citations. questions.stem: A 58-year-old woman with warm autoimmune hemolytic anemia requires pretransfusion ABO/Rh typing before an urgent transfusion. When the technologist tests the patient's RBCs, a saline (negative control) tube shows visible agglutination even before any typing reagent is added, and repeated washing of the cells with warm (37°C) saline fails to disperse the clumping. The pattern is consistent with a potent IgM autoantibody producing spontaneous, direct agglutination of the patient's own cells, obscuring interpretation of the forward ABO/Rh type. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step to obtain a valid, interpretable typing result?

options.text: Treat the patient's RBCs with 0.01 M dithiothreitol (DTT) before repeating the typing
Perform a saline replacement technique on the reaction to disperse the agglutinates
Wash the RBCs repeatedly with warm (37°C) saline until the autoantibody is removed
Add polyethylene glycol (PEG) to the typing reaction to strengthen agglutination
Elute the antibody from the RBCs and retest the eluate against reagent cells

questions.explanation_correct: IgM antibodies are large, pentameric molecules joined by a J-chain, which allows them to directly bridge the distance between RBCs and cause spontaneous, saline-phase (direct) agglutination without requiring the antiglobulin phase — unlike "incomplete" IgG antibodies, which are too small to bridge that gap and require an AHG reagent to be detected. When a potent IgM autoantibody causes this kind of spontaneous agglutination, ordinary warm washing does not remove it. The source material specifies that treating the RBCs with 0.01 M DTT is necessary to obtain a valid type in this situation; DTT cleaves the pentameric J-chain of IgM, breaking up the multivalent antibody lattice responsible for the direct agglutination and allowing accurate antigen typing to proceed.

option_explanations.A: Correct: DTT cleaves the IgM pentamer's J-chain, eliminating the multivalent structure responsible for spontaneous direct agglutination; the source states DTT treatment of the RBCs is necessary for valid typing in this scenario.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: Saline replacement disperses rouleaux (pseudo-agglutination) but does not disperse true antibody-mediated agglutination; since this is genuine IgM-mediated agglutination, saline replacement will not resolve the interference.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect (board trap): The source explicitly notes that spontaneous IgM-mediated agglutination is 'not prevented by warm washing,' so repeated 37°C washing will not remove the interfering autoantibody.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: PEG is an enhancement medium that concentrates antibody to increase reactivity during antibody screening/identification (indirect antiglobulin testing); it strengthens agglutination rather than removing unwanted spontaneous agglutination, so it would worsen rather than solve the typing interference.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect: Elution dissociates antibody from the RBC membrane into a fluid to determine antibody specificity (e.g., for HDFN or hemolytic transfusion reaction workups); it does not treat the cells to remove the interfering IgM and so does not resolve the spontaneous agglutination affecting the type.

questions.teaching_point: IgM antibodies (e.g., potent warm autoantibodies) can directly agglutinate RBCs in saline, independent of the antiglobulin phase, because their pentameric J-chain structure bridges the distance between cells — unlike 'incomplete' IgG antibodies, which are too small to do so and require the AHG (indirect antiglobulin) test. When spontaneous IgM-mediated agglutination interferes with routine typing, warm washing and saline replacement will not resolve it (saline replacement only disperses rouleaux); DTT treatment, which cleaves the IgM J-chain, is the technique needed to obtain a valid type.

source_references.locator: Node 23, Source-Grounded Teaching Pearls: 'Spontaneous Agglutination'
source_references.supports: States that IgM warm autoantibodies can cause spontaneous agglutination not prevented by warm washing, and that treatment of RBCs with 0.01 M DTT is necessary for valid typing.

source_references.supports: Explains that DTT/2-ME cleave the pentameric J-chain of IgM (differentiating IgG from IgM) and that saline replacement disperses rouleaux but not true antibody-mediated agglutination.

source_references.supports: Establishes that IgG ('incomplete') antibodies are too small to bridge the gap between RBCs in saline and require AHG, framing the contrast with IgM's capacity for direct agglutination; also describes PEG as an antibody-concentrating enhancement medium.
