questions.stem:
A 5-year-old girl is brought to the emergency department with fever, pallor, and dark red-brown urine that began after she played outside in cold weather, about one week after recovering from a nonspecific viral upper respiratory infection. She has never received a blood transfusion. Laboratory evaluation reveals an acute drop in hemoglobin along with hemoglobinemia and hemoglobinuria. A Donath-Landsteiner test is performed and is positive.

Which of the following best describes the antibody class and mechanism responsible for this patient's hemolysis?

options.text: IgM autoanti-I that agglutinates RBCs in the cold and produces hemolysis, as in cold agglutinin syndrome
IgG autoanti-P biphasic hemolysin that binds RBCs in the cold, fixes complement, and causes intravascular hemolysis upon rewarming to 37°C
IgG antibody that reacts optimally at 37°C and produces extravascular RBC destruction, as maternal IgG antibodies do in hemolytic disease of the fetus and newborn
Complement-mediated intravascular hemolysis from a deficiency of the GPI-anchored regulatory proteins CD55 and CD59, independent of antibody binding, as in paroxysmal nocturnal hemoglobinuria
A potent alloanti-H antibody causing rapid intravascular hemolysis, as seen when Bombay phenotype recipients are transfused with ordinary group O red cells

questions.explanation_correct: The clinical picture — an acute hemolytic episode with hemoglobinemia/hemoglobinuria precipitated by cold exposure in a child recovering from a viral illness, confirmed by a positive Donath-Landsteiner test — is classic for paroxysmal cold hemoglobinuria (PCH). The approved source states that PCH is associated with autoanti-P, described specifically as "an IgG biphasic hemolysin that binds in the cold and lyses cells upon warming (Donath-Landsteiner test)." The antibody attaches to RBCs and fixes early complement components while the patient is cold; when the blood recirculates and rewarms to 37°C, the complement cascade completes and produces intravascular hemolysis. This two-step ("biphasic") IgG-and-complement mechanism is the key testable concept, and it is a common board trap because cold-reactive antibodies are often reflexively assumed to be IgM.

option_explanations.A: Incorrect. This describes cold agglutinin syndrome (CAS), which the source links to autoanti-I and specifically labels as a typically IgM antibody causing agglutination-related hemolysis or Raynaud-like symptoms — a distinct entity from the IgG biphasic hemolysin process confirmed here by a positive Donath-Landsteiner test.
option_explanations.B: Correct. This matches the source's description of PCH: an IgG biphasic hemolysin (autoanti-P) that binds RBCs in the cold, fixes complement, and causes intravascular hemolysis upon rewarming, which is what the Donath-Landsteiner test detects.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. This describes the extravascular, warm-reactive IgG mechanism the source attributes to maternal IgG alloantibodies in HDFN (destruction at 37°C, extravascular), not the cold-dependent, complement-fixing, intravascular biphasic mechanism confirmed by the Donath-Landsteiner test in this patient.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. This describes paroxysmal nocturnal hemoglobinuria (PNH), where the source explains that loss of GPI-anchored CD55/CD59 causes chronic complement-mediated intravascular hemolysis independent of antibody binding. This patient's hemolysis is antibody-driven (autoanti-P, positive Donath-Landsteiner test), unlike the antibody-independent membrane defect of PNH.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect. This describes the mechanism of hemolysis in Bombay-phenotype (Oh) individuals, whose potent alloanti-H causes rapid intravascular hemolysis when H-bearing red cells are transfused — an alloimmune transfusion reaction, not the autoimmune cold-triggered biphasic hemolysin process described in this vignette.

questions.teaching_point: Paroxysmal cold hemoglobinuria is caused by autoanti-P, an IgG (not IgM) biphasic hemolysin: it binds RBCs and fixes complement in the cold, then causes intravascular hemolysis when the blood rewarms to 37°C, detected by the Donath-Landsteiner test. This distinguishes PCH from IgM-mediated cold agglutinin syndrome (autoanti-I), warm IgG extravascular destruction (as in HDFN), antibody-independent complement dysregulation (PNH), and alloimmune intravascular hemolysis from potent alloanti-H (Bombay phenotype).

source_references.locator: Clinical Presentations / Common Board Traps sections
source_references.supports: PCH is associated with autoanti-P, an IgG biphasic hemolysin that binds RBCs in the cold and lyses them on warming, detected by the Donath-Landsteiner test.

source_references.locator: Clinical Presentations section
source_references.supports: Cold agglutinin syndrome involves autoanti-I, typically an IgM antibody causing hemolysis or Raynaud-like symptoms, used as the CAS distractor.

source_references.locator: Clinical Presentations section

questions.stem: A newborn is delivered to a woman with a documented history of maternal anti-D alloimmunization from a previous pregnancy (antibody titer greater than 4, consistent with active immunization rather than passively acquired anti-D from Rh immune globulin). The infant is jaundiced with anemia at birth, findings consistent with Rh hemolytic disease of the fetus and newborn. Testing on the infant's red cells shows: Rh(D) typing is negative with anti-D reagent, but the direct antiglobulin test (DAT) performed with anti-IgG is strongly positive (4+). Which of the following is the most appropriate next step to determine the infant's true Rh(D) status?

options.text: Perform an eluate from the infant's red cells and test the eluate against a panel of reagent red cells to identify the specificity of the antibody coating the cells
Treat the infant's red cells with EDTA-glycine acid (EGA) to dissociate the bound antibody and repeat the Rh(D) typing on the treated cells
Report the infant's Rh(D) type as negative based on the initial testing and proceed with routine newborn care
Repeat the DAT on the infant's red cells using anti-C3d reagent in place of anti-IgG
Administer Rh immune globulin (RhIG) to the mother based on the infant's DAT result

questions.explanation_correct: This is the classic "blocked Rh" phenomenon: in severe Rh HDFN, maternal IgG anti-D can coat essentially all of the fetal/newborn D antigen sites, occupying them so heavily that the direct Rh(D) typing reagent cannot bind and the type falsely reads as negative, even though the infant is actually D-positive. The strongly positive DAT with anti-IgG (4+) paired with a negative D type is the recognized trigger for this workup. The source-endorsed resolution is to prepare an eluate from the infant's red cells (dissociating the bound antibody into solution) and test that eluate against a reagent red cell panel; identifying anti-D specificity in the eluate confirms that the cells are coated with anti-D (i.e., are actually D-positive) rather than being a true D-negative infant.

option_explanations.A: Correct: elution dissociates the bound IgG into a fluid (eluate) for testing against reagent cells; the sources specifically describe this step as how a 4+ DAT with a D-negative typing result ("blocked Rh") is resolved and the coating antibody's specificity (anti-D) is confirmed.
option_explanations.B: EGA also dissociates IgG from DAT-positive red cells, but its stated purpose is to allow the treated cells themselves to be re-phenotyped for other antigens, and it destroys Kell system antigens in the process. The sources specifically identify elution (eluate testing), not EGA cell treatment, as the method used to resolve the blocked Rh discrepancy.
option_explanations.C: Reporting the type as D-negative without further investigation is exactly the pitfall the "blocked Rh" concept warns against; a strongly positive DAT accompanying a negative Rh type in a newborn with clinical HDFN must be investigated rather than accepted at face value, since it affects downstream management.
option_explanations.D: The DAT was already performed with anti-IgG, the reagent the sources identify as the most important serologic test for HDFN diagnosis. Switching to anti-C3d does not dissociate or identify the specificity of the antibody occupying the D sites and does not resolve the typing discrepancy.
option_explanations.E: RhIG dosing decisions depend on first establishing the infant's true D status, which is the very question this workup is trying to answer; giving RhIG does not identify the antibody coating the infant's cells or clarify whether the negative D type is a true result or a blocked Rh artifact.

questions.teaching_point: A strongly positive DAT (anti-IgG) paired with a false-negative D typing in a newborn with HDFN represents the "blocked Rh" phenomenon (heavy maternal anti-D coating fetal D sites); resolve it by preparing an eluate from the infant's cells and testing the eluate against reagent cells to reveal the antibody specificity and true D-positive status, distinct from EGA/CDP cell treatment used for phenotyping DAT-positive cells.

source_references.supports: Blocked Rh is a false-negative newborn Rh type caused by heavy maternal anti-D coating all D sites, resolved by performing an eluate; positive DAT with anti-IgG is part of the standard newborn HDFN workup.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman is scheduled for an elective hysterectomy tomorrow and may require RBC transfusion. She delivered a full-term infant 6 weeks ago; she received no blood products during that delivery. A type and screen specimen was collected and resulted 5 days ago: Group A, D-positive, antibody screen negative. The surgical team now asks the blood bank to use this specimen to prepare crossmatched units for tomorrow's case. According to specimen requirements for pretransfusion compatibility testing, what is the most appropriate action?

options.text: Proceed with the crossmatch using the existing specimen, since the antibody screen was negative and no new pregnancy or transfusion has occurred since it was drawn
Reject the specimen and request a fresh sample, collected within 3 days of the anticipated transfusion, because she was pregnant within the past 3 months
Request two specimens drawn at separate times to confirm her ABO group before crossmatching
Issue group O, D-negative RBCs empirically rather than relying on the existing specimen
Compare the current result to her historical record and proceed without redrawing, since the ABO/Rh matches prior records

questions.explanation_correct: For patients who have been transfused or pregnant within the preceding 3 months, the pretransfusion specimen used for compatibility testing must be no more than 3 days old. This patient delivered 6 weeks ago, placing her within that 3-month window, so her 5-day-old specimen exceeds the allowed age and a fresh sample must be collected before crossmatched units can be prepared, regardless of the prior negative antibody screen.

option_explanations.A: Incorrect. A negative antibody screen on the existing specimen does not exempt it from the sample-age rule; because she was pregnant within the last 3 months, the specimen must be no more than 3 days old, and this one is 5 days old.
option_explanations.B: Correct. The specimen exceeds the maximum allowed age for a patient transfused or pregnant within the last 3 months, so a new specimen collected within 3 days of the anticipated transfusion is required.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. The two-sample rule described in the source is used to confirm the ABO group when no historical record exists before issuing non-group O units; it does not address or substitute for an expired specimen in a patient with a known prior type.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. Empiric issue of group O RBCs is the described approach when an ABO discrepancy cannot be resolved before an urgent transfusion; this is an elective, non-urgent case with no discrepancy, and it does not correct the underlying problem of an outdated specimen.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect. Comparing current results with historical records is used to detect discrepancies or a history of significant antibodies, but it does not override the requirement for a specimen collected within 3 days for a patient transfused or pregnant in the preceding 3 months.

questions.teaching_point: Pretransfusion specimens for compatibility testing must be no more than 3 days old when the patient has been transfused or pregnant within the preceding 3 months, reflecting the risk that recent immune exposure may have changed the patient's antibody status since any earlier sample was drawn.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man is undergoing ABO typing before elective surgery. Forward (cell) typing shows agglutination of his RBCs with both anti-A and anti-B reagents, a pattern consistent with group AB. Reverse (serum) typing shows his serum agglutinates both reagent A1 cells and reagent B cells, a pattern expected for group O. A fresh sample is redrawn and retested, and the same discordant forward/reverse pattern is reproduced. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step to resolve this ABO discrepancy?

options.text: Wash the patient's RBCs with saline warmed to 37°C and repeat the forward type
Finalize and report the patient as group AB based on the forward-typing result
Conclude the patient has Bombay (Oh) phenotype and select group O, Oh-negative red cells
Attribute the extra forward reactivity to acquired B phenotype from underlying intestinal disease and issue group A red cells
Extend the incubation time of the reverse-typing tubes to enhance reactivity before finalizing the interpretation

questions.explanation_correct: The source material specifically flags cold autoantibodies (e.g., anti-I) as a board trap that can cause spontaneous, nonspecific agglutination of a patient's own RBCs with reagent antisera, producing a forward-typing pattern that mimics group AB even though the reverse type (which reflects the patient's true isoagglutinins) is internally consistent with a different group. Because the discrepancy reproduces on a repeat sample, the source-directed resolution is to wash the RBCs with warm saline to dissociate the cold-reactive autoantibody and then repeat the forward type, rather than reporting an unresolved discrepant type or invoking a different, less-fitting mechanism.

option_explanations.A: Correct. The sources identify cold autoantibodies as a specific cause of spontaneous RBC agglutination that mimics group AB on forward typing, and state this is resolved by warm washing the RBCs before retesting.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. The sources describe a structured discrepancy-resolution process (repeat testing, clinical history, washing, or extended incubation) used specifically to resolve forward/reverse mismatches before a type is finalized, rather than reporting a type that is internally discordant.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. The sources describe Bombay (Oh) phenotype as presenting as a group O discrepancy, where forward typing shows no reactivity with anti-A/anti-B and a potent anti-H reacts with all group O reagent cells on reverse typing — a different pattern from this vignette, where the forward type itself shows reactivity with both anti-A and anti-B.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. The sources list acquired B phenotype (associated with intestinal disease) as one possible cause of extra reactivity in ABO discrepancies, but this patient has no described intestinal disease, and the source specifically attributes the AB-mimicking, warm-wash-responsive pattern to cold autoantibodies rather than acquired B.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect. The sources describe extended incubation (e.g., at 4°C) as a technique to enhance detection of weak antibodies during discrepancy workups, not as the step used to correct a forward-typing problem caused by spontaneous cold agglutination; cooling further would not address (and could worsen) cold-reactive autoagglutination.

questions.teaching_point: An ABO forward-typing result that reacts with all reagent antisera (mimicking group AB) but is discordant with an internally consistent reverse type should raise suspicion for cold autoagglutination; the source-directed fix is warm saline washing of the RBCs before retesting. This is distinguished from Bombay phenotype (a group-O-pattern forward discrepancy with anti-H reacting against all O cells) and acquired B phenomenon (linked to intestinal disease), which are different named board-trap entities with different clinical clues.

questions.domain: 4. Anemia and Red Blood Cell Transfusion
questions.subdomain: Clinical Outcomes of Anemia and Red Cell Transfusion
questions.stem:
A 68-year-old man is hospitalized for a non-bleeding medical illness. He is hemodynamically stable, asymptomatic, and has no history of active cardiac disease. His hemoglobin is 6.5 g/dL, meeting the restrictive transfusion trigger threshold. The admitting physician enters an order for "2 units PRBCs, transfuse now." As part of the hospital's Patient Blood Management (PBM) program, the transfusion service's utilization review process flags this order before it is filled.

Based on the transfusion practice that data-driven PBM programs are designed to promote, which of the following is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Fill the order for 2 units of PRBCs as written, since the patient's hemoglobin is below the restrictive transfusion threshold
Transfuse 1 unit of PRBCs, then reassess the patient's clinical status and hemoglobin before deciding on any additional units
Order thromboelastography (TEG) to determine whether RBC transfusion is indicated before any product is issued
Withhold RBC transfusion altogether and instead start preoperative iron/erythropoietin therapy
Fill the order as written without any utilization review intervention, since the ordering physician has final authority

questions.explanation_correct: Patient Blood Management (PBM) programs are multidisciplinary efforts designed to limit unnecessary transfusion and optimize patient care. The sources specifically describe that data-driven PBM programs (illustrated by the University of Tennessee Medical Center example) work to shift culture away from routine "2-unit blanket orders" toward single-unit RBC orders with clinical reassessment before any additional unit is given. For a hemodynamically stable, asymptomatic patient without active bleeding or cardiac disease, transfusing one unit and then reassessing hemoglobin and clinical status reflects this evidence-based, restrictive-trigger-driven approach, which reduces unnecessary transfusion exposure and helps preserve blood inventory.

option_explanations.A: Incorrect. Automatically filling a two-unit order without reassessment is exactly the traditional "2-unit blanket order" practice that data-driven PBM programs are described as moving away from in favor of single-unit ordering based on clinical status.
option_explanations.B: Correct. This reflects the single-unit-order, reassess-before-more approach that PBM/utilization review programs promote instead of routine two-unit orders, reducing unnecessary transfusion risk and preserving inventory.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. The sources describe TEG/TEM as a PBM tool used to guide transfusion of specific coagulation factors (e.g., plasma, cryoprecipitate, platelets) rather than empirical dosing of those products — not as a tool to determine whether RBC transfusion is indicated for anemia.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. Preoperative iron/erythropoietin is listed as a PBM method for optimizing patients, generally in the elective/preoperative setting, to reduce the future need for transfusion — it does not substitute for RBC transfusion in a patient who already meets the restrictive threshold in the acute inpatient setting described.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect. This describes bypassing utilization review, but the sources indicate utilization committees and CPOE-based utilization review exist specifically to audit physician ordering practices and enforce evidence-based guidelines — deferring without any review contradicts the PBM process being described in the vignette.

questions.teaching_point: Restrictive transfusion triggers (Hb <7 g/dL in stable patients) combined with single-unit ordering and clinical reassessment — rather than routine two-unit blanket orders — are core, source-documented outcomes of Patient Blood Management programs, reducing unnecessary transfusion exposure and preserving blood inventory.

source_references.locator: Domain 2: Clinical Practice & Patient Management (Nodes 16-35); Methods of PBM & Academic Trends
source_references.supports: States RBC hemotherapy clinical trigger is Hb <7 g/dL in stable patients, and that PBM programs (University of Tennessee example) reduce 2-unit blanket transfusions in favor of single-unit orders and reassessment; also lists preoperative iron/EPO as a separate PBM optimization method.

source_references.supports: Describes PBM as multidisciplinary, states data-driven programs favor single-unit orders over traditional 2-unit blanket orders to reduce patient risk and preserve inventory, describes TEG/TEM as guiding transfusion of specific coagulation factors, and describes utilization committees auditing physician ordering practices.

review.notes:
Core facts check out against the four sources:
- Hb <7 g/dL restrictive trigger in stable patients: verbatim in SRC-043 ("RBC Hemotherapy: Clinical triggers (Hb <7 g/dL in stable patients)").
- PBM shifting culture from "2-unit blanket transfusions" to single-unit orders + assessment, illustrated by University of Tennessee Medical Center: verbatim in SRC-043 ("Clinical Impact: As demonstrated by the University of Tennessee Medical Center (Figure 1-4), PBM programs drastically reduce '2-unit blanket transfusions,' shifting the culture toward single-unit orders and assessment") and echoed in SRC

questions.domain: 4. Anemia and Red Blood Cell Transfusion
questions.stem:
A 74-year-old woman with iron deficiency anemia from chronic occult gastrointestinal blood loss has a hemoglobin concentration of 6.9 g/dL that developed gradually over several months. She is asymptomatic at rest, is normotensive, and has an elevated resting cardiac output. Laboratory testing shows an elevated red cell 2,3-diphosphoglycerate (2,3-DPG) concentration.

Which of the following best explains how the elevated 2,3-DPG concentration contributes to this patient's physiologic tolerance of her degree of anemia?

options.text: It shifts the oxyhemoglobin dissociation curve to the right, facilitating oxygen release to the tissues
It shifts the oxyhemoglobin dissociation curve to the left, causing hemoglobin to hold oxygen more tightly and impairing its release to tissues
It increases cardiac output, which is the mechanism by which elevated 2,3-DPG improves tissue oxygenation
It redistributes blood flow away from vital organs so that peripheral tissues receive more oxygen
It reproduces the red cell storage lesion, in which reduced 2,3-DPG improves oxygen delivery to tissue

questions.explanation_correct: Chronic anemia triggers several distinct compensatory mechanisms, including increased cardiac output, redistribution of blood flow to vital organs, and increased red cell 2,3-DPG. Elevated 2,3-DPG specifically shifts the oxyhemoglobin dissociation curve to the right, which facilitates oxygen release (unloading) to the tissues at any given oxygen tension — directly offsetting the reduced oxygen-carrying capacity from the low hemoglobin concentration. This is the opposite effect of a left-shifted curve, which increases hemoglobin's oxygen affinity and impairs tissue delivery (as occurs with decreased 2,3-DPG, e.g., in stored blood).

option_explanations.A: Correct. Increased 2,3-DPG causes a rightward shift of the oxyhemoglobin dissociation curve, which facilitates oxygen release to the tissues — one of the body's recognized compensatory adaptations to anemia.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect and reversed. A leftward shift with increased oxygen affinity and impaired tissue release is the effect of DECREASED 2,3-DPG, not increased 2,3-DPG; this option describes the wrong direction of the curve shift for this patient.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect — conflates two separate compensatory mechanisms. Increased cardiac output and increased 2,3-DPG are independently listed adaptations to anemia; 2,3-DPG improves oxygen delivery via the dissociation curve shift, not by increasing cardiac output.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect and reversed. The recognized compensatory mechanism is redistribution of blood flow TOWARD vital organs, not away from them, and this redistribution is a separate mechanism from the 2,3-DPG-mediated curve shift.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect. The red cell storage lesion is characterized by DECREASED 2,3-DPG (a left shift that impairs oxygen delivery), which is the opposite of this patient's elevated 2,3-DPG and its oxygen-releasing (rightward-shifting) effect.

questions.teaching_point: Physiologic compensation for chronic anemia involves three distinct mechanisms — increased cardiac output, increased red cell 2,3-DPG, and redistribution of blood flow to vital organs. Elevated 2,3-DPG works specifically by shifting the oxyhemoglobin dissociation curve rightward, facilitating tissue oxygen unloading; this is the mechanistic opposite of the storage lesion in banked RBCs, where depleted 2,3-DPG left-shifts the curve and impairs oxygen delivery.

questions.domain: 4. Anemia and Red Blood Cell Transfusion
questions.stem: A 68-year-old man is post-operative day 1 after elective total hip arthroplasty. He is hemodynamically stable (heart rate 78/min, blood pressure 118/74 mmHg), ambulating with physical therapy without difficulty, and denies chest pain, dyspnea, or lightheadedness. He has no history of coronary artery disease. His hemoglobin is 7.4 g/dL (preoperative value 13.0 g/dL), intraoperative blood loss was within expected limits, and there is no evidence of ongoing bleeding. The surgical team orders 2 units of RBCs "to bring the hemoglobin back up to a normal value of 12-13 g/dL." As the transfusion medicine consultant asked to weigh in, what is the most appropriate basis for deciding whether RBC transfusion is indicated in this patient?

options.text: Approve transfusing the 2 units as ordered, because a hemoglobin of 7.4 g/dL is below the normal reference range and should be corrected to a normal value

Base the decision on the patient's clinical need for increased oxygen-carrying capacity (symptoms or physiologic evidence of impaired tissue oxygenation), not on reaching a specific numerical hemoglobin target
Calculate a Corrected Count Increment (CCI) before and after transfusion to objectively confirm that the RBC transfusion achieved adequate efficacy
Recommend transfusing RBCs together with plasma to expand intravascular volume and help support the rise in hemoglobin
Withhold RBC transfusion permanently, because asymptomatic postoperative anemia can never become an indication for transfusion regardless of any later change in the patient's clinical status

questions.explanation_correct: The sources state that RBC transfusion decisions should be driven by the patient's need for increased oxygen-carrying capacity rather than by simply reaching a numerical hemoglobin target. This patient is hemodynamically stable, ambulating, asymptomatic, and has no ongoing blood loss, so a fixed order to "normalize" the hemoglobin to 12-13 g/dL by giving 2 units is not efficacy-based reasoning. Efficacy should instead be assessed against the patient's physiologic need for oxygen delivery, with reassessment guiding any further transfusion.

option_explanations.A: Incorrect. This reflects a numeric-trigger-only approach that the sources explicitly warn against: transfusion decisions should not be based merely on reaching a numerical hemoglobin target, especially in an asymptomatic, hemodynamically stable patient.
option_explanations.B: Correct. The sources state that transfusion decisions should be based on the patient's need for increased oxygen-carrying capacity rather than merely reaching a numerical hemoglobin target, which is precisely how RBC transfusion efficacy should be judged in this stable, asymptomatic patient.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. The Corrected Count Increment (CCI) is described as a standardized formula to evaluate platelet transfusion efficacy, not RBC efficacy; applying it to an RBC transfusion decision misapplies a platelet-specific metric.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. The sources specifically flag that plasma should never be used for simple volume expansion, so co-administering plasma to "support" a hemoglobin rise is a recognized board-trap distractor, not an efficacy strategy.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect. This is an overly rigid absolute ("never," "permanently") not supported by the sources, which frame transfusion need as dependent on the patient's evolving clinical status rather than an outright prohibition in all asymptomatic anemia.

questions.teaching_point: RBC transfusion efficacy is judged by whether it meets the patient's physiologic need for oxygen-carrying capacity (symptoms, hemodynamics, ongoing losses), not by chasing a specific hemoglobin number; this parallels the fact that CCI is a platelet-specific efficacy metric, not an RBC one, and that plasma is never appropriate for volume expansion.

questions.stem: A 52-year-old man is undergoing a scheduled therapeutic phlebotomy procedure (removal of a unit of whole blood) in the blood donor center. Partway through the collection, he becomes pale and diaphoretic and reports feeling light-headed and nauseated. He does not lose consciousness, but he is noted to be hypotensive and bradycardic relative to his baseline vital signs. The phlebotomy is stopped, his legs are elevated, and he is given oral fluids; he recovers fully within several minutes and requires no further medical care. Which of the following best characterizes this adverse event?

options.text: Vasovagal reaction
Citrate toxicity
Nerve injury/irritation from the venipuncture
A Grade 4 (life-threatening, anaphylaxis-type) reaction
Hematoma at the venipuncture site

questions.explanation_correct: This presentation — pallor, diaphoresis, light-headedness, hypotension, and bradycardia during a blood-removal procedure, resolving quickly with simple supportive measures (stopping the draw, leg elevation, oral fluids) and no further medical care — is the classic vasovagal reaction. The approved source material identifies vasovagal reactions as the single most common adverse event of blood-collection procedures and describes them precisely by this triad of hypotension, bradycardia, and (in more severe cases) syncope. Because the event resolved with minimal/no intervention and did not limit the patient's subsequent activities, it also corresponds to the lowest end of the AABB/ISBT severity grading scale, reinforcing that this is a routine, low-severity reaction rather than one of the other listed adverse events.

option_explanations.A: Correct: the described triad of pallor/diaphoresis, hypotension, and bradycardia with rapid resolution on minimal supportive care matches the source's description of vasovagal reactions as the most common adverse event of blood-removal procedures.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: citrate toxicity is specific to apheresis procedures, where citrate anticoagulant used to process and return blood components chelates calcium; it classically causes tingling/perioral paresthesias or shivering and is resolved with oral calcium, not the hypotensive/bradycardic pattern seen in a simple whole-blood phlebotomy.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: nerve injury results from direct needle contact with a nerve during venipuncture and produces localized nerve-type symptoms at the site; per the severity grading framework such symptoms must persist beyond six months to even reach Grade 3, which does not match this patient's systemic, rapidly resolving presentation.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: a Grade 4 reaction requires immediate intervention to prevent death (e.g., anaphylaxis requiring intubation); this patient recovered fully with only minimal supportive measures and needed no urgent intervention, placing the event at a much lower severity.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect: a hematoma is a localized collection of blood/bruising at the venipuncture site rather than a systemic reaction, and would not explain this patient's pallor, diaphoresis, hypotension, and bradycardia.

questions.teaching_point: Vasovagal reactions are the most common adverse event associated with blood-removal procedures (donation or therapeutic phlebotomy alike) and present with hypotension, bradycardia, and sometimes syncope; they are distinguished from citrate toxicity (apheresis-specific, calcium chelation), nerve injury (localized, needle-related), and higher AABB/ISBT severity grades (which require escalating levels of medical intervention) by their symptom pattern and rapid resolution with simple supportive measures.

questions.stem: A transfusion service is preparing to bring a new apheresis instrument into clinical use for collecting hematopoietic progenitor cells by apheresis (HPC-A) to support an upcoming transplant program. The validation team has already confirmed that the instrument was installed according to the manufacturer's specifications and that the collection suite has the appropriate utilities and space. The team is now designing a set of test collections specifically intended to challenge the device at the outer limits of its operating parameters, in order to confirm that the collection process still performs as intended before the medical director will approve the instrument for clinical collections. Which phase of process validation does this "worst-case" testing represent?

options.text: Installation Qualification (IQ)
Operational Qualification (OQ)
Performance Qualification (PQ)
Routine quality control (QC) monitoring
Process verification without full validation

questions.explanation_correct: Operational Qualification (OQ) is the validation phase defined in the sources as demonstrating that a process operates as intended, specifically through "worst-case" challenges that test the process at the limits of its parameters. This is exactly the deliberately stressed, outer-limit test-collection design described in the stem, and it occurs after IQ (which confirms correct installation and appropriate utilities/space, already completed in the scenario) and before PQ (which confirms consistent performance under normal, not worst-case, conditions).

option_explanations.A: Incorrect. Installation Qualification verifies that equipment is installed per manufacturer specifications and that the environment (utilities/space) is appropriate — the stem states this confirmation already occurred before the outer-limit challenge testing began.
option_explanations.B: Correct. OQ is defined as demonstrating the process operates as intended through "worst-case" challenges that test the process at its parameter limits, which matches the test-collection design in the stem.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. Performance Qualification demonstrates that the process consistently performs under normal working conditions in the actual production/clinical environment, not under the deliberately stressed, worst-case conditions described.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. Quality control is testing or observation to determine whether a process is working as expected at a given time, used for ongoing monitoring after a process is implemented. Validation proves a process CAN work reliably; QC monitoring proves it IS still working — it is not part of the pre-implementation validation protocol being described.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect. The sources' decision logic reserves verification alone for processes whose output is easily and cost-effectively checked 100% of the time and that carry lower patient risk. A new apheresis device collecting cellular therapy products for a transplant program is high-risk, so it must be fully validated (or the process redesigned to mitigate risk), not merely verified.

questions.teaching_point: When new apheresis technology/equipment is brought into clinical use (e.g., for HPC-A collection), it must pass through IQ (correct installation/environment) → OQ (worst-case challenge testing of process limits) → PQ (consistent performance under normal working conditions), with final medical director sign-off before clinical implementation; high-risk processes require full validation rather than simple verification.

source_references.supports: Defines the IQ/OQ/PQ validation phases (OQ specifically involves 'worst-case' challenges to test process limits), defines HPC-A as apheresis-based collection of hematopoietic progenitor cells, and states the medical director must approve the validation summary before clinical use.
Provides matching IQ/OQ/PQ definitions and the decision logic that high-risk processes must be fully validated (rather than simply verified) when risk to the patient is high.
Corroborates the same three-phase validation framework, including OQ's focus on 'worst-case challenges' and PQ's focus on normal working conditions in the clinical environment.

questions.stem: A 29-year-old man who meets all standard donor eligibility criteria undergoes an automated 2-unit (double) red blood cell collection by apheresis at the donor center. The procedure is uncomplicated, and his post-donation vital signs are normal. Three weeks later he calls the donor center, asking to donate again because a family member urgently needs blood. Based on donor eligibility requirements for red cell collection, which of the following is the most accurate response regarding his current eligibility to donate red blood cells?

options.text: He is eligible to donate red blood cells now, since only 8 weeks must separate any two red-cell-containing donations
He is not yet eligible; a minimum of 16 weeks (112 days) must elapse after a 2-unit RBC apheresis collection before he may next donate red blood cells
He is permanently deferred from further red blood cell donation because two units were collected by apheresis in a single visit
He is eligible to donate now, because an automated 2-unit RBC apheresis collection does not remove any additional red cell mass compared with a single whole-blood donation

questions.explanation_correct: Donor eligibility standards set a longer inter-donation interval for automated 2-unit (double) red blood cell apheresis collection than for a standard whole-blood donation. Whole blood donation requires an 8-week (56-day) interval between donations, but because a 2-unit RBC apheresis collection removes red cell mass equivalent to two whole-blood donations in a single automated procedure, the donor must wait a minimum of 16 weeks (112 days) before the next red cell donation. This distinction — 8 weeks for whole blood versus 16 weeks for 2-unit RBC apheresis — is a core "principles and technology of donor apheresis" concept: automated centrifugation-based collection technology allows more red cell mass to be harvested per visit, and eligibility intervals are extended accordingly to protect donor safety (adequate erythropoietic recovery).

option_explanations.A: Incorrect. Eight weeks (56 days) is the standard minimum interval between whole-blood donations, not the interval that applies after a 2-unit RBC apheresis collection. Applying the whole-blood interval here understates the recovery time needed after removing two units of red cells in one automated collection.
option_explanations.B: Correct. Because the 2-unit RBC apheresis procedure removes red cell mass equivalent to two whole-blood donations, donor eligibility standards require a minimum 16-week (112-day) interval before the donor may next donate red blood cells, whether by whole blood or apheresis.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. Permanent deferral from donation is reserved for specific findings such as confirmed HBsAg positivity or a history of CJD, not for having undergone a routine, uncomplicated 2-unit RBC apheresis collection. An extended but finite interval (16 weeks), not permanent deferral, applies here.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. The extended interval exists precisely because 2-unit RBC apheresis removes twice the red cell mass of a single whole-blood donation in one sitting; this greater red cell loss is the reason the interval is lengthened rather than left unchanged.

questions.teaching_point: Automated 2-unit (double) RBC apheresis collection removes red cell mass equivalent to two whole-blood donations in one visit, so donor eligibility standards extend the inter-donation interval to 16 weeks (112 days), versus the standard 8-week (56-day) interval for a single whole-blood donation.

questions.stem: A 24-year-old man is donating platelets by apheresis for the first time. About 45 minutes into the procedure he reports new tingling around his mouth and in his fingertips. His vital signs are stable, he is not diaphoretic or pale, and there is no bleeding or swelling at the venipuncture site. Which of the following is the most likely explanation for this donor's symptoms?

options.text: Citrate anticoagulant returned to the donor is chelating ionized calcium, producing hypocalcemia
A vasovagal reaction triggered by the venipuncture
An acute hemolytic reaction from blood re-entering the donor through the apheresis circuit
Air embolism from the apheresis instrument tubing
Hypovolemia from the extracorporeal blood volume in the apheresis circuit

questions.explanation_correct: The approved curriculum source identifies citrate toxicity, manifesting as hypocalcemia, as a common adverse effect of apheresis procedures. During apheresis, citrate anticoagulant mixes with the donor's blood in the extracorporeal circuit, and the fraction of citrate returned to the donor binds (chelates) ionized calcium. The resulting hypocalcemia classically produces perioral and acral paresthesias, exactly as described in this donor, making citrate-induced hypocalcemia the best explanation among the options.

option_explanations.A: Correct: the source explicitly names citrate toxicity presenting as hypocalcemia as a common apheresis adverse effect, and perioral/acral paresthesias are the hallmark finding of hypocalcemia in this setting.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: a vasovagal reaction is a distinct donor reaction typically marked by pallor, diaphoresis, and hypotension/bradycardia rather than isolated perioral and fingertip tingling, and it is not the adverse effect the source attributes to the apheresis process itself.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: this describes a hemolytic transfusion-type reaction, which is not the mechanism the source associates with apheresis donation; donor apheresis returns the donor's own cells, not a mismatched product, so a hemolytic mechanism does not fit.
option_explanations.D: Air embolism is a mechanical apheresis-circuit complication, not the adverse effect the source identifies as common, and it would not selectively produce circumoral and acral paresthesias with otherwise stable vitals.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect: extracorporeal-volume-related hypovolemia would be expected to cause lightheadedness or hypotension rather than the paresthesias described, and it is not the adverse effect the source highlights for apheresis donors.

questions.teaching_point: Citrate anticoagulant used during apheresis procedures binds ionized calcium; the returned citrate can cause donor hypocalcemia, classically presenting with perioral and acral paresthesias. This must be distinguished from a vasovagal reaction, which presents instead with pallor, diaphoresis, and hypotension/bradycardia.

source_references.locator: Domain IV: Apheresis — High-Yield Facts
source_references.supports: States that a common adverse effect of apheresis procedures is citrate toxicity, presenting as hypocalcemia.

questions.domain: 6. Hazards of Transfusion: Specific Adverse Events
questions.stem: A 68-year-old man undergoes elective hip surgery and receives 2 units of crossmatch-compatible RBCs intraoperatively. He has no fever, chills, pain, or hypotension during or immediately after the transfusion. On postoperative day 6 he is more fatigued than expected for his surgery and appears mildly jaundiced; his urine remains clear. Laboratory testing shows a hemoglobin level lower than expected for his clinical course and an elevated indirect bilirubin. A direct antiglobulin test (DAT) on a current sample is positive, and the antibody eluted from his red cells was not detectable on his pretransfusion antibody screen. Which of the following best describes the mechanism responsible for this patient's findings?

options.text: An anamnestic (secondary) IgG alloantibody response causing extravascular clearance of transfused red cells
Preformed IgM antibody activating complement and causing intravascular hemolysis after an ABO-incompatible transfusion
Mechanical shear injury to red cells from infusion through an undersized needle
Bacterial contamination of the transfused unit causing transfusion-associated sepsis
Autoantibody-mediated destruction of both transfused and autologous red cells as part of a sickle cell hyperhemolysis process

questions.explanation_correct: The timing (postoperative day 6, i.e., within the 24-hour-to-28-day window), a newly positive DAT, an eluate revealing an alloantibody that was undetectable on the pretransfusion screen, and the absence of hemoglobinuria are the classic pattern of a delayed hemolytic transfusion reaction (DHTR). DHTR arises from an anamnestic (secondary) immune response: the patient was previously sensitized to a foreign RBC antigen (e.g., from prior transfusion or pregnancy), but the antibody had fallen below detectable titers at the time of pretransfusion testing. Re-exposure to the antigen on transfused cells triggers rapid IgG antibody production, and the antibody-coated donor cells are cleared extravascularly by splenic/hepatic macrophage Fc and C3b receptors. This is the source-cited contrast with acute hemolytic transfusion reaction (AHTR), which is typically intravascular, IgM/complement-mediated, and occurs immediately/within 24 hours.

option_explanations.A: Correct: matches the source-defined DHTR pattern — positive DAT 24 hours to 28 days post-transfusion with a newly identified alloantibody, mediated by IgG and cleared extravascularly, in contrast to the immediate, intravascular, IgM/complement-mediated mechanism of AHTR.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: this describes AHTR, which is usually intravascular, IgM/complement-mediated, and presents acutely (during/within 24 hours of transfusion) with fever, chills, pain, hypotension, and hemoglobinuria — not a day-6 presentation with clear urine. Clerical error causing ABO incompatibility is the classic cause of this acute intravascular pattern, not a delayed presentation.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: mechanical destruction from small needles, pumps, or improper temperature is a cause of nonimmune hemolysis, which characteristically produces a negative DAT — inconsistent with this patient's positive DAT and newly identified eluate antibody.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: transfusion-associated bacterial sepsis presents with high fever (rise of >2°C), rigors, and profound hypotension typically during or very shortly after transfusion of the contaminated unit, not as a delayed anemia/jaundice picture with a positive DAT and eluate-identified alloantibody days later.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect: hyperhemolysis syndrome is a rare, devastating reaction described specifically in sickle cell disease in which both donor and autologous red cells are destroyed, requiring avoidance of further transfusion; there is no indication this patient has sickle cell disease, and this distinct process differs from the alloantibody-eluate pattern described here.

source_references.supports: Defines DHTR as positive DAT 24h-28 days post-transfusion with newly identified alloantibody/positive eluate; states AHTR is intravascular/IgM/complement/immediate while DHTR is extravascular/IgG/days later; describes AHTR clinical picture (fever, chills, pain, hypotension) and nonimmune hemolysis with negative DAT from mechanical causes; describes TTBI presentation (high fever, rigors, hypotensio

States that clerical errors remain the primary, often preventable, cause of acute intravascular hemolytic reactions, supporting why ABO-incompatible/ clerical-error hemolysis presents acutely rather than in a delayed fashion.

Describes transfusion-associated sepsis (TAS) presenting with high fever and profound hypotension typically during or shortly after transfusion of a contaminated unit, used to support why bacterial contamination does not fit this delayed presentation.
Confirms hemolytic reactions (including delayed) are a core subdomain under the Hazards of Transfusion domain in the board curriculum, establishing topical relevance.

review.notes: Core testable fact is well-grounded: SRC-045 explicitly defines DHTR (positive DAT 24h-28 days post-transfusion, newly identified alloantibody/eluate) and explicitly contrasts it with AHTR (intravascular/IgM/immediate) vs DHTR (extravascular/IgG/delayed) -- this single fact cleanly discriminates option A from B-E, and no other option is defensible, so single_best_answer=true and independent_answer=A matches the key. questions.domain: 6. Hazards of Transfusion: Specific Adverse Events
questions.stem: A 45-year-old man is transfused an RBC unit that, on later review, had been intended for a different patient with the same last name. About 15 minutes into the infusion he spikes a temperature of 39.4°C, has shaking chills, and passes dark red-brown urine. Over the next several hours his urine output falls sharply, and he repeatedly tells the nurse that he feels like something terrible is about to happen. Which of the following best describes the underlying process producing this presentation?

options.text: Acute intravascular hemolysis caused by ABO-incompatible RBC transfusion
Hypocalcemia from citrate in the transfused unit chelating the patient's ionized calcium
Hyperkalemia from potassium that accumulated in the unit's supernatant during storage
A febrile nonhemolytic reaction triggered by cytokines released from residual leukocytes in the unit

questions.explanation_correct: The symptom complex described — fever, chills, hemoglobinuria (dark red-brown urine), evolving renal failure (falling urine output), and a subjective "sense of impending doom" — is the classic presentation of acute (intravascular) hemolysis from ABO incompatibility. The setup (a unit meant for another patient) also reflects that clerical/identification error is the leading, largely preventable cause of these acute intravascular hemolytic reactions.

option_explanations.A: Correct: fever, chills, hemoglobinuria, renal failure, and a sense of impending doom are the described hallmark features of acute intravascular hemolysis from ABO incompatibility, and a wrong-unit (clerical) error is the classic preventable trigger.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: citrate-induced hypocalcemia presents with perioral paresthesias, tingling, shivering, tetany, or arrhythmias from rapid infusion of citrated components — not with hemoglobinuria, fever/chills, or renal failure.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: hyperkalemia from RBC storage supernatant manifests as cardiac arrhythmia or arrest with rapid infusion, particularly in at-risk patients — it does not explain dark urine, oliguria, or the impending-doom sensation described here.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: febrile nonhemolytic reactions from leukocyte-derived cytokines cause fever and chills (which is why leukoreduction is used to reduce them), but they do not cause hemoglobinuria or renal failure, which point instead to intravascular RBC destruction as in option A.

questions.teaching_point: Recognize the classic sign/symptom complex of an acute (intravascular) hemolytic transfusion reaction — fever, chills, hemoglobinuria, renal failure, and a sense of impending doom, typically from ABO incompatibility due to a clerical/identification error — and distinguish it from other transfusion-related complications (citrate-induced hypocalcemia, storage-related hyperkalemia, febrile nonhemolytic reaction) that share overlapping features like fever or cardiac symptoms but lack the hemolysis-specific findings.

source_references.locator: Clinical Presentations / Prevention sections
source_references.supports: ABO incompatibility can present as acute intravascular hemolysis characterized by fever, chills, hemoglobinuria, renal failure, and a feeling of impending doom; leukoreduction decreases febrile nonhemolytic reactions.

source_references.locator: Hemovigilance / Massive Transfusion Protocol sections
source_references.supports: Clerical errors remain the primary, largely preventable cause of acute intravascular hemolytic reactions; citrate toxicity from rapid infusion causes hypocalcemia (perioral paresthesias, tetany, arrhythmias); hyperkalemia from RBC storage supernatant can cause cardiac arrest with rapid infusion.

source_references.supports: Citrate toxicity presents as perioral paresthesias, tingling in the extremities, or shivering, with severe cases progressing to tetany or cardiac arrhythmias.

questions.domain: 6. Hazards of Transfusion: Specific Adverse Events
questions.stem:
A 62-year-old man is evaluated 7 days after receiving 2 units of RBCs for gastrointestinal bleeding. His pretransfusion antibody screen had been negative. He now reports fatigue and mild jaundice, and his hemoglobin is lower than expected for his clinical course. The direct antiglobulin test is positive for IgG and C3d. An eluate is prepared from his red cells and tested against a standard reagent antibody panel; in parallel, his serum is retested against the same panel and against an autocontrol (his own red cells). Both the eluate and the serum react with every reagent panel cell tested, as well as with the patient's own red cells in the autocontrol.

Which of the following is the most likely explanation for this reactivity pattern?

options.text: Delayed hemolytic transfusion reaction from an anamnestic anti-Jka (Kidd) response
A warm-reactive autoantibody rather than a red cell alloantibody
Acute hemolytic transfusion reaction from ABO incompatibility
An antibody directed at a low-prevalence antigen present on the donor red cells
A dosage effect of a Kidd antibody reacting preferentially with homozygous-expressing reagent cells

questions.explanation_correct: Reactivity with every reagent cell AND the autocontrol is the classic pattern of a warm autoantibody, not a specific alloantibody. A true alloantibody is identified by inclusion/exclusion logic: it reacts only with cells carrying the corresponding antigen and leaves antigen-negative cells (and usually the autocontrol) nonreactive. Because this patient's eluate and serum are panreactive against the entire panel as well as his own cells, the pattern points to a broadly reactive warm autoantibody (as seen in warm autoimmune hemolytic anemia) rather than to a delayed hemolytic transfusion reaction from a defined alloantibody.

option_explanations.A: Incorrect. Kidd antibodies are classically linked to rapid titer decline and anamnestic delayed hemolytic transfusion reactions, making the 7-day timing and prior negative screen tempting — but a genuine anti-Jka alloantibody is identified by exclusion (nonreactivity with Jka-negative cells) and would not typically make the autocontrol positive. Universal reactivity of every panel cell plus the autocontrol argues against a single defined alloantibody.
option_explanations.B: Correct. Panreactivity of the eluate/serum against all panel cells together with a positive autocontrol is the pattern the sources describe for a warm autoantibody, distinguishing it from a simple (alloantibody-mediated) hemolytic process.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. Acute hemolytic transfusion reactions from ABO incompatibility occur acutely (typically during or shortly after transfusion, reflecting a clerical/ABO mismatch), not as a process unfolding a week later, and this option does not account for the described panreactive eluate/autocontrol pattern.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. An antibody to a low-prevalence antigen is invoked to explain an incompatible crossmatch despite a NEGATIVE antibody screen — the opposite scenario from this patient, whose screen and eluate are diffusely positive against essentially the entire panel.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect. A dosage effect produces selective, often weak reactivity limited to homozygous (double-dose) antigen-positive cells, with heterozygous or antigen-negative cells nonreactive — not uniform strong reactivity with every cell in the panel plus the autocontrol.

source_references.locator: Clinical Presentations; Definitions; Common Board Traps and Distractors
source_references.supports: States that reactivity of all panel cells and the autocontrol indicates a likely warm autoantibody, and defines exclusion (rule-out) as failure to react with antigen-negative cells, plus the Kidd/Duffy dosage-only reactivity board trap and the low-prevalence-antigen explanation for incompatible crossmatch with a negative screen.

source_references.supports: States that in WAIHA the eluate typically reacts with all cells tested (panreactive), distinguishing it from simple alloantibodies, and gives the common DAT IgG+C3d pattern.

source_references.supports: Associates Kidd (Jk) antibodies with rapid antibody disappearance and delayed hemolytic transfusion reactions, and classifies AHTR (ABO) versus DHTR (anamnestic Jk/Kidd) in the transfusion reaction table.

questions.domain: 6. Hazards of Transfusion: Specific Adverse Events
questions.stem:
A hospitalized patient's red blood cell transfusion is stopped after the nurse suspects an acute hemolytic transfusion reaction. The partially transfused unit with attached tubing, along with a freshly drawn post-transfusion patient sample, is sent to the transfusion service. The transfusion service medical director directs staff to manage this event according to the standard nonconforming event (NCE) investigation framework used for transfusion-related adverse reactions.

Which of the following is the most appropriate immediate step in the laboratory investigation of this suspected hemolytic transfusion reaction?

options.text: Secure (quarantine) the implicated unit in-house while beginning the investigation, including reviewing records to reconstruct how the event occurred
Submit a Biological Product Deviation report to the FDA immediately, before quarantining the unit or reviewing any records
Perform root cause analysis and implement corrective action before the investigation into the event has been completed
Discard the partially transfused unit and tubing right away, since testing on a partially used unit will not change patient management
Skip formal documentation and severity classification of the event and proceed directly to interviewing staff, since documentation can be completed afterward

questions.explanation_correct: The source's nonconforming event (NCE) framework, explicitly used to "detect, investigate, and resolve errors or adverse reactions," lays out an ordered sequence: Documentation (capture facts, classify severity) → Impact Determination (evaluate effect on product safety/purity/potency) → Quarantine/Recall ("secure affected products in-house or initiate a recall for distributed products") → Investigation ("interview staff and review records to reconstruct the event") → Root Cause Analysis → CAPA → Reporting → Effectiveness Review. Applied to a suspected acute hemolytic transfusion reaction, the appropriate immediate laboratory step is to secure the implicated unit and begin reconstructing the event through record review, before advancing to root cause analysis or external regulatory reporting.

option_explanations.A: Correct. This mirrors the source's stated sequence: Quarantine/Recall ("secure affected products in-house") occurs alongside/after Impact Determination, and Investigation ("interview staff and review records to reconstruct the event") is the next defined step in the NCE framework, both of which precede root cause analysis, CAPA, and reporting.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. Per the source, Reporting is one of the final steps in the NCE sequence (after documentation, quarantine, investigation, root cause analysis, and CAPA). Additionally, a Biological Product Deviation report is only required once an event meets all five defined criteria (manufacturing-associated, cGMP deviation, may affect safety/purity/potency, occurred under facility control, and involved a distributed product) — this determination itself requires investigation first, not before it.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. The source's NCE framework places Investigation (interviewing staff, reviewing records) before Root Cause Analysis and Corrective/Preventive Action (CAPA); reversing this order skips the fact-gathering needed to identify the true root cause.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. The framework calls for securing (quarantining) affected products in-house, not discarding them, so they remain available for the investigation; premature disposal removes evidence needed to reconstruct the event.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect. Documentation, including capturing the facts and classifying the event by risk severity, is listed as the first step in the NCE process, not something deferred until after staff interviews.

questions.teaching_point: When a suspected hemolytic transfusion reaction is reported, the transfusion service should follow the nonconforming event framework in order — document and classify severity, determine potential impact on product safety, quarantine (not discard) the implicated product, and investigate via staff interview and record review — before proceeding to root cause analysis, corrective action, or external regulatory reporting such as a Biological Product Deviation report.

questions.domain: 6. Hazards of Transfusion: Specific Adverse Events
questions.stem:
A 68-year-old man is receiving his second unit of red blood cells for symptomatic anemia. Ninety minutes into the transfusion, his temperature rises from 37.0°C to 38.2°C and he reports mild chills. He denies dyspnea, flank or back pain, and any sense of impending doom. His blood pressure, heart rate, and oxygen saturation are unchanged from pre-transfusion values, and there is no jugular venous distension. The transfusion is stopped, a clerical recheck confirms the correct patient and unit, and the post-transfusion plasma sample shows no visible hemoglobinemia. The direct antiglobulin test is negative, and repeat ABO/Rh typing matches the pretransfusion type.

Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

options.text: Acute hemolytic transfusion reaction
Transfusion-associated circulatory overload (TACO)
Febrile nonhemolytic transfusion reaction
Transfusion-related acute lung injury (TRALI)
Transfusion-transmitted bacterial infection (septic reaction)

questions.explanation_correct: Febrile nonhemolytic transfusion reaction (FNHTR) is defined as a temperature rise of ≥1°C occurring during or within 4 hours of transfusion that is not attributable to hemolysis or another cause, and is mechanistically linked to cytokines. This patient's isolated fever and chills, with stable hemodynamics, no respiratory compromise, and a negative hemolysis workup (normal plasma inspection for hemoglobinemia, negative DAT, matching repeat ABO/Rh type), fits this definition once the other acute reactions have been excluded by their characteristic findings.

option_explanations.A: Incorrect: acute hemolytic transfusion reaction classically presents with fever plus chills, flank/back pain, hypotension, and a feeling of impending doom, with laboratory hallmarks of hemolysis (hemoglobinuria, decreased haptoglobin, elevated LDH/bilirubin, spherocytes) and typically a positive DAT. This patient has none of the pain or hypotension and a negative hemolysis workup, arguing against AHTR.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: TACO presents with dyspnea, hypertension, tachycardia, jugular venous distension, and an S3 gallop from volume overload. This patient has no respiratory symptoms, no hypertension, and no JVD, making TACO unlikely.
option_explanations.C: Correct: FNHTR is a ≥1°C temperature rise during or within 4 hours of transfusion not attributable to hemolysis or another cause, related to cytokines. The isolated fever/chills with stable vitals and negative hemolysis workup match this definition of exclusion.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: TRALI requires new acute respiratory distress with hypoxemia (oxygen saturation <90% on room air), often with hypotension, occurring within 6 hours of transfusion. This patient has no respiratory distress and an unchanged oxygen saturation.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect: bacterial contamination/septic transfusion reactions are characterized by a high fever (≥2°C rise), rigors, and profound hypotension, particularly with room-temperature-stored platelets. This patient's more modest 1.2°C rise, absence of rigors, and stable blood pressure make a septic reaction less likely than FNHTR.

questions.teaching_point: FNHTR is a diagnosis of exclusion defined by a ≥1°C temperature rise during or within 4 hours of transfusion, mediated by cytokines, and distinguished from AHTR, TRALI, TACO, and septic/TTBI reactions by the absence of their respective hallmark findings (hemolysis evidence/pain/hypotension for AHTR, hypoxemic respiratory distress for TRALI, volume-overload signs for TACO, and high-grade fever with rigors and profound hypotension for septic reactions).

source_references.supports: Defines FNHTR as a ≥1°C temperature rise during/within 4 hours of transfusion not attributable to hemolysis; describes the clinical presentations, diagnostic workup (plasma inspection, DAT, repeat ABO typing), and distinguishing features of AHTR, TRALI, TACO, and bacterial/septic (TTBI) reactions used to differentiate them from FNHTR.
States that febrile reactions are mediated by cytokines, in contrast to IgA-deficiency-mediated allergic reactions, supporting the mechanism cited in the explanation.

Classifies FNHTR (cytokines) alongside AHTR, allergic/anaphylactic, and TRALI reactions as acute immune transfusion reactions in the reaction-classification table, supporting the differential set used in the vignette.

review.notes: Core diagnostic logic is well grounded. SRC-045 line 19 gives the exact FNHTR definition used ("rise in temperature (≥1°C) during or within 4 hours of transfusion not attributable to hemolysis or other causes"), and the stem's ruling-out findings map cleanly onto SRC-045's explicit presentation/workup bullets: AHTR ruled out via absence of flank/back pain, hypotension, "impending doom" (line 25) and a negative hemolysis workup (plasma hemoglobinemia inspection, DAT, repeat ABO grouping — line 37); TACO ruled out via absence of dyspnea, hypertension, JVD (line 29); TRALI ruled out via unchanged

questions.domain: 6. Hazards of Transfusion: Specific Adverse Events
questions.stem: While receiving a unit of fresh frozen plasma, a patient develops facial flushing, diffuse urticaria, wheezing, and hypotension within minutes of the infusion starting. She remains afebrile throughout the episode. The transfusion is stopped immediately and supportive management is initiated. Which of the following underlying conditions is most likely responsible for this reaction?

options.text: IgA deficiency with anti-IgA antibodies in the recipient
Accumulation of donor leukocyte-derived cytokines in the stored component
Passive transfer of donor antibodies against recipient HLA or neutrophil antigens
Bacterial contamination of the transfused unit
ABO incompatibility between donor and recipient

questions.explanation_correct: The vignette describes a severe, life-threatening allergic reaction with multi-organ involvement (respiratory: wheezing; cardiovascular: hypotension) developing rapidly after the start of a plasma-containing transfusion, without fever — the classic anaphylactic transfusion reaction picture. Anaphylactic reactions represent the severe end of the allergic reaction spectrum and are classically linked to IgA-deficient recipients who have developed anti-IgA antibodies that react against IgA in donor plasma, triggering the rapid multi-organ presentation, in contrast to the cytokine-mediated mechanism of febrile nonhemolytic reactions.

option_explanations.A: Correct. Severe/anaphylactic allergic transfusion reactions are classically attributed to IgA deficiency with preformed anti-IgA antibodies reacting against donor plasma IgA, producing this rapid-onset, multi-organ (respiratory/cardiovascular) presentation.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. Cytokines accumulating in stored components from donor leukocytes are the mechanism behind febrile nonhemolytic transfusion reactions, which present with a temperature rise rather than urticaria, wheezing, and hypotension.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. Passive transfer of donor anti-HLA/anti-HNA antibodies is the mechanism of TRALI, which classically presents with fever plus hypoxemia and noncardiogenic pulmonary edema, not the afebrile urticarial/wheezing picture described here.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. Bacterial contamination (transfusion-transmitted bacterial infection) classically causes a high fever with rigors and profound hypotension, particularly with room-temperature-stored platelets; this patient is afebrile and received plasma.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect. ABO incompatibility causes an acute hemolytic transfusion reaction, typically presenting with fever, chills, and flank/back pain from intravascular hemolysis, not the urticarial/wheezing presentation described.

questions.domain: 6. Hazards of Transfusion: Specific Adverse Events
questions.stem:
A hospital's hemovigilance committee reviews a transfusion-related fatality that occurred after a patient received a red blood cell unit. Chart review shows the unit was infused faster, and in a volume, than the patient's cardiovascular status could accommodate. The reaction was not recognized as transfusion-related at the time it occurred and was identified only later, during root-cause review of the death — consistent with hemovigilance data showing that this category of noninfectious complication is frequently underrecognized and underreported. Along with TRALI, this type of event is among the leading causes of transfusion-related fatality reported to the FDA.

Which of the following best explains the mechanism responsible for this fatal reaction?

options.text: Excessive volume and/or rate of blood component infusion relative to the patient's cardiovascular capacity
New acute lung injury developing within 6 hours of the transfusion
Engraftment and proliferation of viable donor T lymphocytes because the component was not irradiated
ABO-incompatible transfusion resulting from a clerical error in patient or sample identification

questions.explanation_correct: The vignette describes a fatal reaction driven by the volume and infusion rate of the component relative to the patient's cardiovascular capacity, which the sources identify as the defining mechanism of Transfusion-Associated Circulatory Overload (TACO). The sources also note that, together with TRALI, TACO is among the leading causes of transfusion-related fatality reported to the FDA, and that TACO (like TRALI) is a noninfectious complication that is frequently underrecognized and underreported by hemovigilance systems — both features reflected in the vignette's description of a reaction identified only on root-cause review.

option_explanations.A: Correct: the sources define TACO as an adverse reaction to the volume and/or rate of component infusion, and list it alongside TRALI as a leading, frequently underrecognized/underreported cause of transfusion-related fatality — matching the vignette exactly.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: this describes TRALI, which the sources define as new acute lung injury within 6 hours of transfusion. TRALI is also a leading reported cause of fatality, making it a plausible distractor, but the vignette's clue is an infusion volume/rate problem, not a defined acute lung injury syndrome.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: this describes TA-GVHD, which the sources state is prevented by gamma irradiation of cellular components to inactivate donor T lymphocytes. Nothing in the vignette concerns irradiation status or donor lymphocyte engraftment.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: this describes an acute hemolytic transfusion reaction from ABO incompatibility, which the sources identify as classically caused by clerical error. The vignette gives no indication of a typing/identification error, only a volume/rate issue.

TRALI, TACO, and hemolytic reactions are the most frequent transfusion-related fatalities reported to the FDA; TACO and TRALI are noninfectious complications that are often underrecognized and underreported; clerical errors remain the primary cause of acute intravascular hemolytic reactions.

questions.domain: 6. Hazards of Transfusion: Specific Adverse Events
questions.stem: A 52-year-old woman undergoes elective hip arthroplasty and receives 2 units of fresh frozen plasma for intraoperative coagulopathy. Approximately 90 minutes after the plasma infusion begins, she develops acute respiratory distress with severe hypoxemia (oxygen saturation <90% on room air, PaO2/FiO2 ratio ≤300 mm Hg), a temperature of 38.6°C, and a blood pressure drop from 118/76 to 82/54 mm Hg. A portable chest radiograph obtained at that time shows new bilateral pulmonary infiltrates that were not present preoperatively. She has no history of cardiac or pulmonary disease, and her B-type natriuretic peptide (BNP) level is essentially unchanged from her preoperative baseline. The transfusion is stopped immediately, and other causes of acute lung injury are being actively excluded. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Administer intravenous furosemide and reassess her respiratory status
Resume the plasma transfusion at a slower infusion rate once she is hemodynamically stable
Provide aggressive respiratory support, including mechanical ventilation if needed, and avoid administering diuretics
Withhold supplemental oxygen until donor HLA and HNA antibody testing confirms the diagnosis
Place the patient in an upright seated position, restrict further intravenous fluids, and repeat BNP testing

questions.explanation_correct: This presentation — new bilateral infiltrates with hypoxemia (PaO2/FiO2 ≤300 mm Hg), fever, and hypotension arising within hours of a plasma transfusion, with a normal/unchanged BNP — fits Transfusion-Related Acute Lung Injury (TRALI) rather than TACO. TRALI is a diagnosis of exclusion once noncardiogenic bilateral pulmonary edema is confirmed, and management is aggressive respiratory support (often mechanical ventilation); diuretics are specifically avoided because they can worsen TRALI-associated hypotension.

option_explanations.A: Incorrect. Diuretics such as furosemide are TACO therapy; in TRALI they are avoided because they can worsen the hypotension characteristic of this reaction rather than help it.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. Unlike a simple urticarial reaction, pulmonary transfusion reactions (TRALI/TACO) require permanent cessation of the implicated unit and a full workup — the unit should never be restarted.
option_explanations.C: Correct. TRALI management is aggressive respiratory support (up to mechanical ventilation) with avoidance of diuretics, since diuretics can worsen the hypotension seen in TRALI.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. TRALI diagnosis and treatment are driven by the clinical picture; donor HLA/HNA antibody testing supports later donor mitigation/deferral, not acute bedside management, and respiratory support must not be delayed for it.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect. Upright positioning with fluid restriction is the TACO management pathway; it does not fit this patient, whose hypotension (rather than hypertension) and unchanged BNP argue against volume overload as the mechanism.

questions.teaching_point: TRALI presents with acute hypoxemia, fever, and hypotension within hours of transfusion with new bilateral infiltrates and a normal/near-normal BNP (distinguishing it from TACO's hypertension and elevated BNP); it is a diagnosis of exclusion, and acute management is aggressive respiratory support with avoidance of diuretics, plus permanent cessation of the implicated unit — donor antibody workup is for later donor mitigation, not acute treatment.
