SRC-004 (Node 45, 'Confidentiality & Rights'): 'Donor Rights: Donors have the right to be informed of their testing results and to be treated with a standard of care that prevents injury (e.g., nerve damage during phlebotomy)' — this maps almost word-for-word onto option A. SRC-004/SRC-006 also both state Battery = performing a procedure with no consent at all, Strict Liability is 'rarely applied to blood in most states,' and Informed Consent requires explaining risks/benefits/alternatives (not just a signature) — these ground

questions.stem:
A 34-year-old man is brought to the trauma bay in hemorrhagic shock after a high-speed motor vehicle collision. The massive transfusion protocol is activated, and over the next 15 minutes he rapidly receives multiple units of red blood cells, plasma, and platelets through a rapid infuser. Shortly afterward, he develops perioral tingling, numbness of his fingertips, and shivering. His temperature is normal, he has no hypertension or jugular venous distension, he is not in respiratory distress and remains normoxic, and there is no urticaria or wheezing.

Which of the following is the most likely cause of these findings, and what is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Citrate toxicity from rapid infusion of citrate-anticoagulated blood components; slow the transfusion rate and give calcium supplementation
Transfusion-related acute lung injury; begin aggressive respiratory support and avoid diuretics
Transfusion-associated circulatory overload; place the patient upright and administer diuretics
Acute hemolytic transfusion reaction; stop the transfusion and give vigorous fluid resuscitation to maintain urine output
Anaphylaxis; stop the transfusion and treat as a severe, multi-organ allergic reaction

questions.explanation_correct: This patient's symptom pattern-perioral paresthesias, tingling fingertips, and shivering without fever, hemodynamic instability, hypoxemia, or urticaria-is the classic presentation of citrate toxicity, which occurs when large volumes of citrate-anticoagulated blood components are infused rapidly, as in a massive transfusion protocol for hemorrhagic shock. Citrate chelates ionized calcium, producing these neuromuscular symptoms and, if untreated, tetany or arrhythmias. Management is to slow the infusion rate and provide calcium supplementation, which allows the transfusion to safely continue rather than being stopped outright.

option_explanations.A: Correct: the symptom triad of perioral tingling, fingertip numbness, and shivering matches the defined presentation of citrate toxicity, and slowing the rate plus calcium supplementation is the specified management.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: TRALI presents with sudden respiratory distress, hypoxemia, fever, and hypotension-none of which this patient has; his oxygenation and breathing are normal, arguing strongly against TRALI.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: TACO presents with dyspnea, hypertension, tachycardia, JVD, and an S3 gallop; this patient has none of these volume-overload findings, and diuretics would not address his actual symptoms.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: AHTR presents with fever, chills, flank/back pain, and hypotension, and its management centers on maintaining urine output; this patient is afebrile and hemodynamically unchanged, and his symptoms (paresthesias) are not typical of AHTR.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect: anaphylaxis is a severe, life-threatening reaction with respiratory and cardiovascular involvement (and typically urticaria); this patient has no such multi-organ involvement or skin findings.

questions.teaching_point: During massive transfusion for hemorrhagic shock, rapid infusion of large volumes of citrate-anticoagulated blood components can cause citrate toxicity (perioral paresthesias, tingling, shivering, and, if severe, tetany/arrhythmias) via calcium chelation; this must be distinguished from AHTR, TRALI, TACO, and anaphylaxis by its distinct symptom pattern, and is managed by slowing the transfusion and giving calcium rather than stopping the transfusion outright.

source_references.locator: Clinical Presentations / Management sections
source_references.supports: Citrate toxicity presents with perioral paresthesias, tingling of fingertips, and shivering, and is managed by slowing the infusion rate and giving calcium supplementation.

source_references.locator: Clinical Presentations / Laboratory Findings sections
source_references.supports: Citrate chelates ionized calcium causing perioral paresthesias, tingling, and shivering (severe cases: tetany/arrhythmias); managed with slowed infusion and calcium replacement.

source_references.locator: Clinical Presentations / Management sections
source_references.supports: Defines the comparison presentations used as distractors: TRALI (respiratory distress, hypoxemia, fever, hypotension; treat with respiratory support, avoid diuretics), TACO (dyspnea, hypertension, JVD, S3 gallop; treat with diuretics/upright positioning), AHTR (fever, chills, back pain, hypotension; treat with fluids to maintain urine output), and anaphylaxis (severe multi-organ allergic reaction)

review.notes:
Findings from independent read of all four sources:

1. SYMPTOM PATTERN AND MANAGEMENT (well supported): SRC-047 and SRC-023 both state, nearly verbatim, that citrate toxicity "presents with perioral paresthesias, tingling in fingertips, shivering, and potentially tetany or arrhythmias" and is "managed by slowing the infusion rate and providing calcium supplementation (oral or IV)." This matches the stem's symptom triad and option A's management exactly. The distractor rationales for B (TRALI), C (TACO), D (AHTR), and E (anaphylaxis) are all accurately sourced to SRC-045's defined presentation

questions.stem: A 34-year-old man is brought to the trauma bay after a high-speed motor vehicle collision with a closed pelvic fracture and suspected intra-abdominal hemorrhage. He is hypotensive, tachycardic, and has cool, mottled extremities consistent with ongoing hemorrhagic shock. As resuscitation begins, an arterial blood gas shows a markedly decreased pH consistent with lactic (metabolic) acidosis from tissue hypoperfusion. Which of the following best describes the expected effect of this acidosis on hemoglobin's oxygen affinity and its physiologic consequence for this patient?

options.text: The decreased pH shifts the oxyhemoglobin dissociation curve to the right, lowering hemoglobin's oxygen affinity and promoting oxygen release to the hypoperfused tissues
The decreased pH shifts the oxyhemoglobin dissociation curve to the left, raising hemoglobin's oxygen affinity and impairing oxygen release to the tissues
The acidosis acts primarily by depleting intracellular 2,3-DPG, producing a leftward shift that reduces oxygen unloading at the tissue level
Acidosis does not meaningfully affect hemoglobin's oxygen affinity; curve position in this setting is governed only by body temperature
The physiologic effect described is attributable to the storage lesion of transfused RBC units, which depletes 2,3-DPG and shifts the curve left, impairing oxygen delivery

questions.explanation_correct: The approved sources list decreased pH (acidosis) alongside increased 2,3-DPG, increased CO2, and increased temperature as established causes of a rightward shift of the oxyhemoglobin dissociation curve. A right shift lowers hemoglobin's affinity for oxygen and facilitates oxygen release to the tissues — an adaptive, compensatory response that helps deliver oxygen to hypoperfused tissue during hemorrhagic shock.

option_explanations.A: Correct: per the sources, decreased pH (acidosis) is a direct cause of a right shift, which lowers oxygen affinity and increases oxygen release to tissues, matching the compensatory need in hemorrhagic shock.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: reverses the direction. The sources associate a left shift with increased pH (alkalosis), not decreased pH, and a left shift raises oxygen affinity while impairing (not facilitating) tissue oxygen release — the opposite of the adaptive response needed here.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: conflates two mechanisms the sources list separately. Decreased pH causes a right shift directly; decreased 2,3-DPG is a distinct, separately listed cause of a left shift (and is specifically described as a feature of stored blood), not the mechanism driving this patient's acute lactic acidosis.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: the sources explicitly list pH, along with 2,3-DPG, CO2, and temperature, as determinants of oxyhemoglobin dissociation curve position, so pH is not irrelevant to hemoglobin's oxygen affinity.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect: this describes the storage lesion of transfused stored RBC units (decreased 2,3-DPG causing a left shift, described in the sources as common in stored blood), which is a distinct process from the patient's own systemic lactic acidosis. The patient's endogenous acidosis causes a right shift, not a left shift, so attributing the described physiology to the storage lesion is a mismatch.

questions.teaching_point: In hemorrhagic shock, systemic lactic acidosis (decreased pH) is one of the recognized drivers of a rightward shift of the oxyhemoglobin dissociation curve, an adaptive mechanism that lowers hemoglobin's oxygen affinity and enhances peripheral oxygen unloading. This is mechanistically distinct from the leftward shift caused by 2,3-DPG depletion in stored RBC units (the storage lesion) — a common board trap is conflating the two acid-base/shift phenomena.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 46 – Oxygen Transport & Adaptations, 'Mechanics of the Shift' section
source_references.supports: States that decreased pH (acidosis) causes a right shift facilitating oxygen release, while decreased 2,3-DPG (common in stored blood) and increased pH (alkalosis) cause a left shift that impairs oxygen delivery.

source_references.locator: 'Clinical Presentations' and 'High-Yield Comparison Points — Left Shift Causes' sections
source_references.supports: Confirms right shift occurs with low pH (hypoxia/acidosis) and releases oxygen more easily to tissues; lists decreased 2,3-DPG, decreased temperature, and increased pH (alkalosis) as left shift causes.

review.notes: The tested science is fully and almost verbatim grounded: SRC-002 (Node 46, 'Mechanics of the Shift') states decreased pH (acidosis) causes a right shift that 'facilitates oxygen release to the tissues,' while decreased 2,3-DPG (explicitly flagged as 'common in stored blood'), decreased CO2, decreased temperature, or increased pH (alkalosis) cause a left shift that 'potentially impair[s] tissue delivery.' SRC-005 corroborates this in 'Clinical Presentations' and 'High-Yield Comparison Points.' This cleanly validates option A as correct and B, C, D, E as each identifiably wrong for the reasons

questions.stem: A 24-year-old man is brought to the trauma bay in hemorrhagic shock after a high-speed motor vehicle collision and is placed on a massive transfusion protocol. Over the next 45 minutes he rapidly receives 8 units of RBCs, 8 units of plasma, and 1 apheresis platelet unit through peripheral IV lines without an inline blood warmer. His core temperature falls to 34.2°C, and the surgical team notes new, diffuse oozing from all wound edges and IV insertion sites. Repeat labs show a platelet count of 140,000/µL, fibrinogen 220 mg/dL, and a normal ionized calcium. Which of the following is the most likely explanation for this patient's new bleeding?

options.text: Dilutional coagulopathy from crystalloid-predominant resuscitation
Citrate-induced hypocalcemia impairing the coagulation cascade
Hypothermia impairing platelet function and coagulation enzyme activity
Hyperkalemia-induced cardiac dysfunction
Delayed hemolytic transfusion reaction

questions.explanation_correct: The patient's core temperature of 34.2°C is below the 35°C threshold that defines transfusion-associated hypothermia, produced by rapid infusion of refrigerated blood components without a warmer. Hypothermia exacerbates coagulopathy because cold temperatures inhibit platelet function and slow the enzymatic reactions of the clotting cascade, causing diffuse, nonsurgical oozing even though the platelet count and fibrinogen are not critically depleted. This is the classic physical/metabolic complication of unwarmed rapid massive transfusion in trauma resuscitation.

option_explanations.A: Dilutional coagulopathy results from replacing lost whole blood mainly with RBCs and crystalloid; this patient instead received a balanced 1:1:1 RBC:plasma:platelet ratio, the strategy specifically used to prevent dilutional coagulopathy, and his platelet count/fibrinogen are not depleted, so this is less likely than hypothermia.
option_explanations.B: Citrate toxicity causes hypocalcemia (citrate chelates calcium) with perioral paresthesias, tetany, or arrhythmias, but the stem gives a normal ionized calcium — the correct assay for citrate toxicity — which excludes this mechanism.
option_explanations.C: Correct: rapid transfusion of unwarmed refrigerated components dropped the core temperature below 35°C, and cold temperature directly inhibits platelet function and the enzymatic activity of the coagulation cascade, producing the diffuse oozing described.
option_explanations.D: Hyperkalemia arises from potassium leakage from stored RBCs and presents with peaked T waves and cardiac arrest, not diffuse bleeding, so it does not explain this patient's oozing.
option_explanations.E: Delayed hemolytic transfusion reaction is an anamnestic antibody response occurring 2 to 14 days after transfusion with a hemoglobin drop, jaundice, and fever — not immediate intraoperative oozing during active massive transfusion.

questions.teaching_point: Transfusion-associated hypothermia (core temp <35°C) from rapid infusion of unwarmed refrigerated blood components impairs platelet function and slows coagulation-enzyme kinetics, producing a functional coagulopathy distinct from citrate-induced hypocalcemia, hyperkalemia, dilutional coagulopathy, and delayed hemolytic transfusion reaction; blood warmers are indicated for rapid, large-volume transfusion to prevent this complication.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 34, Definitions/Clinical Presentations/Management
source_references.supports: Defines transfusion hypothermia as core temp <35°C from rapid refrigerated component infusion; states cold temperatures inhibit platelet function and clotting-cascade enzymatic activity, worsening coagulopathy; blood warmers mandatory for rapid large-volume infusion.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 16, Core Concepts/Clinical Presentations

review.notes: All stem premises, the keyed answer, and all five option rationales are fully and precisely grounded in the three approved sources. SRC-060 (Node 34) and SRC-032 (Node 16) both state verbatim that hypothermia (<35°C, from rapid unwarmed refrigerated-component infusion) "inhibits platelet function and the enzymatic activity of the clotting cascade" / "exacerbate[s] coagulopathy by reducing enzymatic activity" — an exact match to the correct-answer mechanism. Distractor eliminations are each independently sourced: dilutional coagulopathy (A) is defined in SRC-032 as arising from RBC+crystalloid-

questions.stem:
A hospital's transfusion-reaction investigation committee is completing its hemovigilance report to the NHSN Biovigilance Module for a case of new-onset dyspnea and radiographic pulmonary edema that developed shortly after a red cell transfusion in a patient with known underlying cardiac disease. On review, the case fulfills several, but not all, of the standardized case-definition criteria for transfusion-associated circulatory overload (TACO). Weighing the patient's cardiac history against the close temporal relationship to the transfusion, the committee concludes that transfusion is the most likely, though not certain, precipitant of the event.

Using the NHSN imputability scale applied to hemovigilance reporting, how should this reaction be classified?

options.text: Definitive
Probable
Possible
Doubtful/Ruled Out
Near-miss event

questions.explanation_correct: The NHSN Biovigilance Module imputability scale grades how likely it is that a reported reaction was actually caused by the transfusion. "Probable" is defined as a reaction that meets some, but not all, of the case-definition criteria, where transfusion is judged the most likely cause. That is exactly the committee's conclusion here: several (not all) TACO criteria are met, and after weighing the clinical picture, transfusion is deemed the most likely precipitant. This is distinct from "Definitive" (all criteria fulfilled), "Possible" (transfusion cannot be excluded but another cause is equally likely), and "Doubtful/Ruled Out" (evidence favors another cause) — and distinct from "near-miss event," which is a separate hemovigilance classification for occurrences that did not actually cause harm.

option_explanations.A: Incorrect. "Definitive" imputability requires that the reaction fulfill all case-definition criteria; this case meets only some of the standardized TACO criteria, so it cannot be called definitive.
option_explanations.B: Correct. "Probable" applies when a reaction meets some but not all case-definition criteria and transfusion is judged the most likely cause — precisely the committee's conclusion in this vignette.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. "Possible" is reserved for reactions where transfusion cannot be ruled out but another cause is considered equally likely; here the committee specifically judged transfusion to be the most likely cause, which is a stronger determination than "possible."
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. "Doubtful/Ruled Out" requires evidence that clearly favors or proves a cause other than transfusion. Nothing in the vignette points to a competing etiology outweighing transfusion as the cause.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect. A near-miss event is a distinct hemovigilance classification for an unexpected occurrence that did not result in harm but could have. This patient had an actual adverse reaction (dyspnea, pulmonary edema), so the event is a completed reaction being graded on the imputability scale, not a near-miss.

questions.teaching_point: The NHSN Biovigilance Module's imputability scale (Definitive, Probable, Possible, Doubtful/Ruled Out) standardizes how likely a reported adverse reaction is attributable to transfusion, and is a separate axis from event-type classifications (incident, nonconformance, near-miss, unanticipated event) used elsewhere in hemovigilance surveillance. Board questions can test whether learners correctly apply imputability criteria to a scenario rather than confusing it with severity grading or event-type terminology.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man who takes clopidogrel after placement of a coronary stent is being evaluated for persistent oozing from a minor skin puncture site made during line placement. His platelet count is within the normal reference range. He has not received any platelet transfusions, and there is no clinical evidence of sepsis, splenomegaly, or disseminated intravascular coagulation. Which of the following best explains why his normal platelet count does not ensure adequate hemostasis?

options.text: Platelet-mediated hemostasis depends on both platelet quantity and platelet quality (function); clopidogrel, a P2Y12 inhibitor, impairs platelet function even with a normal platelet count
Immune-mediated platelet refractoriness from HLA antibodies is impairing his platelet count increment
His fibrinogen level has most likely fallen below the threshold associated with diffuse microvascular bleeding
Subclinical disseminated intravascular coagulation is nonimmune-mediated consumption of his platelets
Splenic sequestration is reducing his circulating platelet mass

questions.explanation_correct: Platelets form the initial physical plug at a site of vascular injury (primary hemostasis), and the source material specifies that platelet hemostatic efficacy depends on both quantity (count) and quality (function) — a normal count does not by itself guarantee adequate hemostasis. Clopidogrel is explicitly identified as a P2Y12 inhibitor that must be discontinued before elective surgery as part of "optimizing coagulation," reflecting its role in impairing platelet function. In this patient, a normal platelet count with ongoing oozing, no transfusion history, and no clinical picture of sepsis, DIC, or splenomegaly is best explained by drug-induced qualitative platelet dysfunction rather than a quantitative platelet or coagulation-factor problem.

option_explanations.A: Correct. The sources tie platelet hemostatic efficacy to both count and function, and separately identify P2Y12 inhibitors such as clopidogrel as agents that impair coagulation/hemostasis and must be stopped before elective surgery — together these explain bleeding despite a normal count.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. Immune-mediated (HLA/HPA antibody) refractoriness describes an inadequate posttransfusion platelet count increment in previously transfused/alloimmunized patients; this patient has not received a platelet transfusion, so refractoriness does not apply.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. Hypofibrinogenemia causes diffuse microvascular bleeding only once fibrinogen falls below the 100-150 mg/dL range; there is no fibrinogen data given, and this describes a distinct coagulation-factor mechanism rather than the antiplatelet-drug effect suggested by the vignette.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. DIC is listed as a nonimmune cause of poor platelet count increments (consumption), but it is typically accompanied by a low platelet count and a systemic clinical picture, neither of which is present here.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect. Splenomegaly/splenic sequestration is listed as a nonimmune cause of poor platelet increments and would be expected to lower the circulating platelet count, but this patient's count is normal.

questions.teaching_point: A normal platelet count reflects only the quantitative side of platelet-mediated (primary) hemostasis; adequate hemostasis also requires normal platelet quality/function, which can be impaired by antiplatelet agents such as P2Y12 inhibitors (e.g., clopidogrel) even when the count is normal.

questions.stem: A 24-year-old woman is evaluated in the transfusion medicine clinic for a lifelong history of easy bruising, gingival bleeding, and heavy menstrual periods dating back to adolescence. Her brother has a similar lifelong bleeding history. She has no history of chronic kidney disease or uremia, has never undergone cardiac surgery or cardiopulmonary bypass, and denies use of aspirin, NSAIDs, or any other antiplatelet medication. Her complete blood count shows a normal platelet count. Based on this history, which of the following is the most likely underlying diagnosis?

options.text: Glanzmann thrombasthenia
Uremia-associated platelet dysfunction
Cardiopulmonary bypass-associated platelet dysfunction
Drug-induced platelet dysfunction
Immune thrombocytopenia (ITP)

questions.explanation_correct: The source material groups platelet function disorders into congenital (naming Bernard-Soulier syndrome and Glanzmann thrombasthenia as examples) and acquired (naming uremia, cardiopulmonary bypass, and drugs as examples). This patient's presentation - lifelong mucocutaneous bleeding, an affected sibling, and the explicit absence of every named acquired trigger (no uremia, no cardiopulmonary bypass, no antiplatelet drug use) plus a normal platelet count (arguing against a destructive process like ITP) - fits the congenital category, making Glanzmann thrombasthenia the best answer among the choices given.

option_explanations.A: Correct. Glanzmann's is explicitly listed among the congenital platelet function disorders in the source material, in contrast to the acquired causes of platelet dysfunction. A lifelong bleeding history with a similarly affected sibling and no exposure to any of the recognized acquired triggers, together with a normal platelet count (arguing against a destructive process), is most consistent with an inherited platelet function disorder.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect (board trap). Uremia is named in the source material as an acquired cause of platelet dysfunction, not a congenital one. This patient has no history of chronic kidney disease or uremia, so this acquired trigger is not present.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect (board trap). Cardiopulmonary bypass is listed as an acquired cause of platelet dysfunction. The patient has never had cardiac surgery, eliminating this acquired etiology.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect (board trap). Drug exposure is listed as an acquired cause of platelet dysfunction. The patient specifically denies use of aspirin, NSAIDs, or other antiplatelet agents, so a drug effect cannot explain her lifelong symptoms.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect. ITP is described as a disorder of platelet destruction for which HLA and HPA antibody assays are used in the workup, i.e., a quantitative/immune-mediated process rather than an inherited platelet function defect. Her normal platelet count argues against a destructive thrombocytopenic process, and ITP is not among the congenital disorders named in the source material.

questions.teaching_point: Genetic/congenital platelet function disorders (e.g., Glanzmann thrombasthenia, Bernard-Soulier syndrome) should be distinguished from acquired causes of platelet dysfunction (uremia, cardiopulmonary bypass, drugs) and from destructive/immune platelet disorders such as ITP, based on history (lifelong onset, family history, absence of acquired triggers) and platelet count.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old man with acute myeloid leukemia and severe neutropenia (absolute neutrophil count 100/µL) develops progressive invasive pulmonary aspergillosis that is not responding to maximal antifungal therapy. The transfusion medicine service arranges for a granulocyte (neutrophil) concentrate to be collected by apheresis from a community donor and prepared for transfusion into this patient. Before this cellular blood component can be released for transfusion, which processing step is required, and what is its purpose?

options.text: Gamma irradiation, to inactivate donor T lymphocytes and prevent transfusion-associated graft-versus-host disease
T-cell depletion, the manipulation used for hematopoietic progenitor cell products
CD34+ selection, the manipulation used to enrich progenitor cells in hematopoietic progenitor cell products
Volume/plasma reduction, the manipulation used for hematopoietic progenitor cell products
CMV-reduced processing, the requirement described for neonatal recipients

questions.explanation_correct: Granulocyte (neutrophil) concentrates are cellular blood components rich in donor leukocytes, including immunocompetent T lymphocytes carried along with the therapeutic neutrophils. The approved source states that transfusion-associated graft-versus-host disease (TA-GVHD) is prevented specifically by gamma irradiation of cellular components, which inactivates donor T lymphocytes so they cannot engraft and attack the recipient. This is the required processing step for a granulocyte concentrate before release. It must be distinguished from manipulations the sources describe for a different product category — hematopoietic progenitor cell (HPC) grafts undergo volume/plasma reduction, CD34+ selection, or T-cell depletion — and from CMV-reduced component processing, which the sources tie specifically to neonatal recipients rather than to this adult patient.

option_explanations.A: Correct. The source states TA-GVHD is prevented by gamma irradiation of cellular components to inactivate donor T lymphocytes; a granulocyte concentrate is exactly this type of lymphocyte-containing cellular product.
option_explanations.B: T-cell depletion is described in the source as a specialized manipulation applied to hematopoietic progenitor cell (HPC) products, not as a processing step for granulocyte concentrates; it is a board trap because it superficially sounds GVHD-related.
option_explanations.C: CD34+ selection enriches stem/progenitor cells and is described in the source as an HPC-specific manipulation; it is irrelevant to a mature neutrophil (granulocyte) product, which contains no progenitor cells to select.
option_explanations.D: Volume/plasma reduction is listed in the source as an HPC-specific specialized manipulation (along with CD34+ selection and T-cell depletion); it addresses volume, not the TA-GVHD risk posed by donor T lymphocytes in a granulocyte concentrate.
option_explanations.E: CMV-reduced components are described in the source as required for neonates (infants under 4 months), not for this adult AML patient; this option misapplies a real but population-specific requirement to the wrong clinical scenario.

questions.teaching_point: Granulocyte (neutrophil) concentrates are cellular components carrying donor T lymphocytes and therefore require gamma irradiation to prevent TA-GVHD; this must be distinguished from processing manipulations reserved for HPC products (T-cell depletion, CD34+ selection, volume reduction) and from CMV-reduced processing, which is a population-specific requirement for neonates rather than the TA-GVHD-prevention step needed here.

questions.stem: A term neonate born to a healthy woman who is blood group O, Rh-positive develops jaundice at approximately 36 hours of life. This is the mother's first pregnancy, and her prenatal antibody screen (performed with reagent group O screening cells) was negative throughout gestation. The infant types as blood group A, Rh-positive. Hemoglobin and hematocrit at birth were within the normal range. A peripheral blood smear shows spherocytes without erythroblastosis, and the infant's direct antiglobulin test (DAT) is positive. Which of the following best explains how this neonate developed hemolytic disease despite a negative maternal antibody screen and no prior pregnancy?

options.text: Anti-A and anti-B are naturally occurring IgG antibodies that do not require a prior sensitizing pregnancy, and they are not detected by an antibody screen performed with group O reagent cells
The mother formed anti-D during this pregnancy, which crossed the placenta and caused hemolysis without any prior sensitizing exposure
Maternal IgM anti-A crossed the placenta and coated the infant's RBCs, producing the positive DAT
The infant is Kell-antigen positive, and maternal anti-K suppressed fetal erythropoiesis independent of any sensitization history
A silent fetomaternal hemorrhage earlier in this pregnancy sensitized the mother to the infant's paternally inherited D antigen

questions.explanation_correct: This presentation (delayed mild jaundice at 12-48 hours, normal birth hemoglobin, spherocytes without erythroblastosis, positive DAT) matches the source-described profile of ABO HDFN, which is typically mild compared with Rh HDFN. Unlike Rh(D) HDFN, ABO HDFN can occur in a first pregnancy because anti-A and anti-B are naturally occurring antibodies and do not require a prior sensitizing pregnancy or transfusion. Because routine prenatal antibody screens use reagent group O cells to detect "unexpected" (non-ABO) alloantibodies, naturally occurring anti-A/anti-B are not flagged by this screen, explaining why the mother's screen remained negative even though ABO-mediated hemolysis occurred in her group A infant.

option_explanations.A: Correct: anti-A/anti-B are naturally occurring and need no prior sensitizing pregnancy, and group O reagent screening cells (used to detect unexpected, non-ABO antibodies) do not carry A or B antigens, so they cannot flag these isoagglutinins.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: Rh alloimmunization (anti-D) requires a prior sensitizing exposure to D-positive cells and would have been detected by the antibody screen; moreover, the mother is Rh-positive and shares the D antigen with the infant, so she cannot form anti-D against it.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: only IgG antibodies (IgG1 and IgG3) are actively transported across the placenta; IgM antibodies do not cross, so IgM anti-A cannot mediate this hemolytic process.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: anti-K-mediated disease is notable for suppression of erythropoiesis rather than the spherocytic hemolytic pattern seen here, and like Rh antibodies, anti-K formation requires a prior sensitizing exposure (transfusion or pregnancy), which this primigravida with a negative screen does not have.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect: because the mother is Rh-positive, she and the infant share the D antigen, so no maternal anti-D sensitization to D is possible; any true Rh alloimmunization would also have produced a positive antibody screen.

questions.teaching_point: ABO HDFN differs fundamentally from Rh HDFN in that anti-A/anti-B are naturally occurring IgG antibodies requiring no prior sensitizing pregnancy, so it can present in a first pregnancy with a negative antibody screen (which uses group O cells and only detects unexpected, non-ABO alloantibodies); it also tends to be clinically milder, with normal birth hemoglobin, delayed jaundice, and spherocytes rather than erythroblastosis.

source_references.supports: ABO HDFN can occur in a first pregnancy because anti-A/anti-B are naturally occurring, unlike Rh HDFN which needs sensitization; only IgG crosses the placenta; ABO HDFN is typically mild with normal birth hemoglobin, delayed jaundice (12-48h), spherocytes (vs erythroblasts in Rh), positive DAT; anti-K suppresses erythropoiesis rather than causing simple hemolysis.
Antibody screens use reagent group O cells to detect unexpected (non-ABO) clinically significant alloantibodies such as anti-K, anti-Fya, anti-Jka.
General domain framing that pathogenesis of ABO and Rh HDFN is a major exam concept within Obstetric and Pediatric Patients.

review.notes: Core clinical/serologic facts in the stem and key rationale are explicitly grounded in SRC-017: (1) "ABO HDFN can occur in the first pregnancy because anti-A and anti-B are naturally occurring" (Common Board Traps); (2) mild ABO HDFN phenotype — normal birth Hgb, delayed jaundice 12-48h, spherocytes rather than erythroblasts, positive DAT (Clinical Presentations / Laboratory Findings / High-Yield Comparison Points); (3) "Only IgG antibodies...are actively transported across the placenta, making them the primary cause of HDFN" (Core Concepts), which correctly kills option C (IgM doesn't cross);

questions.stem: A 15-year-old girl with homozygous sickle cell disease (HbSS) is hospitalized after an acute ischemic stroke. To lower her percentage of hemoglobin S as rapidly as possible and reduce her risk of stroke extension or recurrence, the transfusion service plans an emergent RBC procedure. Routine serologic testing shows the patient types as D-positive. Which of the following is the most appropriate combination of transfusion procedure and pretransfusion RBC testing?

options.text: Automated erythrocytapheresis (red cell exchange) to lower HbS below 30%, selecting donor units only after molecular (genotype-based) testing is used to confirm the patient's true RhD status and support extended antigen matching
Automated erythrocytapheresis (red cell exchange) to lower HbS below 30%, selecting the first ABO/D-compatible units available without further antigen matching
A simple (top-up) RBC transfusion, selecting donor units only after molecular (genotype-based) testing is used to confirm the patient's true RhD status and support extended antigen matching
Therapeutic plasma exchange (TPE) using fresh frozen plasma as replacement fluid, selecting donor units only after molecular (genotype-based) testing
Extracorporeal photopheresis (ECP), selecting the first ABO/D-compatible units available without further antigen matching

questions.explanation_correct: Automated erythrocytapheresis (red cell exchange) is the apheresis procedure used in sickle cell disease to rapidly reduce the percentage of HbS below 30% for stroke prevention. Because SCD patients are recognized as a population at particular risk of harboring Rh variants that type as conventional D by serology but can still form allo-anti-D, molecular/genotype-based RBC testing is essential before selecting donor units, so that an underlying partial D can be identified and the patient managed as Rh-negative for transfusion, preventing sensitization to the missing D epitopes.

option_explanations.A: Correct: red cell exchange targeting HbS below 30% is the source-supported apheresis approach for sickle cell disease, and pairing it with molecular RhD testing addresses the SCD-specific risk that a serologically D-positive patient actually has an undetected partial D variant that could form allo-anti-D if exposed to conventional D-positive units.
option_explanations.B: Exchange transfusion is the correct procedure, but relying on serologic D-typing alone is a board trap: it can miss a partial D variant that appears as conventional D by serology yet still carries a risk of allo-anti-D formation, so extended/molecular matching should not be skipped in SCD patients.
option_explanations.C: A simple top-up transfusion does not accomplish the rapid, targeted reduction in %HbS that erythrocytapheresis provides; the sources specify red cell exchange, not simple transfusion, as the procedure used to lower HbS in sickle cell disease.
option_explanations.D: Therapeutic plasma exchange with FFP replacement is the apheresis therapy used for TTP to remove an ADAMTS13 inhibitor; it is not the modality used to reduce hemoglobin S in sickle cell disease.
option_explanations.E: Extracorporeal photopheresis is used for cutaneous T-cell lymphoma and graft-versus-host disease, not for lowering HbS in sickle cell disease, and this option also omits the antigen matching needed in this population.

questions.teaching_point: In sickle cell disease, automated red cell exchange (erythrocytapheresis) is the apheresis modality used to rapidly lower %HbS (target <30%) for stroke prevention, and molecular RBC genotyping is essential in this population to detect Rh variants that mimic conventional D serologically yet carry allo-anti-D risk, guiding safe donor unit selection and RhIg/transfusion Rh-status decisions.

All five options are clearly differentiable on source grounds and B/C/D

questions.stem: A 5-year-old child with a congenital hemoglobinopathy other than sickle cell disease has been receiving regular red blood cell transfusions since infancy to manage chronic anemia and now presents for a routine follow-up visit. Because this child's transfusion history reflects a large cumulative lifetime exposure to RBC units rather than a reaction from any single transfusion episode, which of the following complications should be the primary focus of long-term surveillance in this patient?

options.text: Iron overload from cumulative transfusional iron loading
Acute hemolytic transfusion reaction from an ABO-identification (clerical) error
Transfusion-related acute lung injury (TRALI)
Transfusion-associated circulatory overload (TACO)
Transfusion-associated graft-versus-host disease (TA-GVHD)

questions.explanation_correct: Iron overload is recognized as one of the major hazards of transfusion, and unlike the other listed complications it is driven by the cumulative burden of repeated RBC transfusions over time rather than by any single transfusion episode. A child with a non-sickle congenital hemoglobinopathy who has required regular transfusions since infancy has substantial cumulative RBC exposure, making progressive iron accumulation the dominant long-term surveillance concern, in contrast to the acute, per-episode hazards represented by the other options.

option_explanations.A: Correct: iron overload is explicitly named among the recognized hazards of transfusion and is the complication that specifically accumulates with a large lifetime burden of RBC transfusions, matching this child's long transfusion history since infancy.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: acute hemolytic transfusion reactions are most commonly caused by a clerical/ABO-identification error occurring at the time of a single transfusion episode, not by the cumulative number of units received over a lifetime.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: TRALI is defined by new acute lung injury occurring within hours of a transfusion — an acute reaction tied to one episode, not a complication that builds with cumulative long-term exposure.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: TACO is a reaction to the volume or infusion rate of an individual transfusion episode, not a consequence of the total number of transfusions accumulated over time.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect: TA-GVHD results from donor T lymphocytes in a transfused cellular product and is prevented by irradiation of components; it is an episode-related risk from a given unit, not the hallmark complication of a large cumulative transfusion burden.

questions.teaching_point: Distinguish cumulative, dose-dependent hazards of long-term transfusion support (iron overload) from acute, single-episode transfusion reactions (TRALI, TACO, TA-GVHD, acute hemolytic reactions from clerical error) — a distinction relevant to any pediatric patient, including those with non-sickle congenital hemoglobinopathies, who requires chronic RBC transfusion.

questions.stem: A female infant is born at 26 weeks' gestation with a birth weight of 890 g; this is the mother's first pregnancy. On day of life 6, a routine complete blood count reveals thrombocytopenia. The infant has no petechiae, ecchymoses, or other evidence of bleeding, and there are no clinical signs of sepsis or necrotizing enterocolitis. The maternal platelet count is normal. The neonatology team requests a platelet transfusion. Which of the following is the most appropriate consideration when the blood bank selects and prepares this platelet unit?

options.text: The unit should be irradiated before issue, because the infant's birth weight is below the threshold at which irradiation is required to prevent transfusion-associated graft-versus-host disease.
HPA-1a–negative platelets should be selected, because this clinical picture is diagnostic of fetal and neonatal alloimmune thrombocytopenia.
Irradiation may be omitted, because irradiation is required only for intrauterine and exchange transfusions, not for simple platelet transfusions in preterm infants.
A standard-volume, non-aliquoted apheresis platelet unit is appropriate, since small-volume aliquots from dedicated donors apply only to red cell transfusions.
Selecting a CMV-reduced-risk unit is sufficient on its own; irradiation can be omitted for platelet transfusions in premature infants.

questions.explanation_correct: Irradiation of cellular blood components is mandatory for intrauterine transfusion, exchange transfusion, and premature neonates weighing less than 1200 g, to inactivate donor T lymphocytes and prevent transfusion-associated graft-versus-host disease (TA-GVHD). This infant, born at 890 g, meets that birth-weight criterion, so the platelet unit must be irradiated regardless of the underlying cause of the thrombocytopenia. Nothing in the vignette (no petechiae, ecchymoses, or intracranial hemorrhage; normal maternal platelet count) fits the classic presentation of fetal and neonatal alloimmune thrombocytopenia (FNAIT), so antigen-specific platelet selection is not supported here; the clearly source-supported action is birth-weight-based irradiation.

option_explanations.A: Correct: the approved source states irradiation is mandatory for premature neonates <1200 g (in addition to IUT and exchange transfusion) to prevent TA-GVHD, and this infant's 890 g birth weight meets that criterion.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: FNAIT classically presents with petechiae, ecchymoses, or life-threatening intracranial hemorrhage. This infant has none of those findings, so there is no basis in the vignette to diagnose FNAIT and reflexively request HPA-1a-negative platelets; note that FNAIT can occur even in a first pregnancy, but the absence of hemorrhagic findings argues against invoking it here.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: the source specifies irradiation is mandatory not only for IUT and exchange transfusion but also for premature neonates <1200 g, which directly applies to this infant's simple platelet transfusion.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: source-based neonatal transfusion practice favors small-volume aliquots from dedicated units to minimize donor exposure as a general neonatal transfusion principle, not one limited to red cell components.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect: CMV-reduced-risk product selection is a separate, additional neonatal blood-banking consideration and does not substitute for the birth-weight-based irradiation requirement, which specifically addresses TA-GVHD prevention rather than CMV transmission.

questions.teaching_point: Premature neonates weighing less than 1200 g require irradiated cellular blood products for any transfusion (not just IUT or exchange transfusion) to prevent TA-GVHD; incidental, non-hemorrhagic thrombocytopenia in a preterm infant should not be reflexively attributed to FNAIT, which classically causes petechiae, ecchymoses, or intracranial hemorrhage.

questions.stem:
A 26-week-gestation infant, born weighing 900 g, is now 5 weeks old and weighs 980 g in the NICU. There is no evidence of hemolysis or blood group incompatibility (DAT negative, no jaundice out of proportion to expected), but over the past two weeks he has developed tachycardia, tachypnea, poor weight gain, and an increasing supplemental oxygen requirement. His hemoglobin has progressively fallen, and his serum erythropoietin level is inappropriately low for the degree of anemia. The neonatology team determines that RBC transfusion is indicated.

Which of the following represents the most appropriate transfusion approach for this infant?

options.text: Transfuse a small-volume aliquot (10–15 mL/kg) of irradiated RBCs from a dedicated donor unit
Perform a double-volume exchange transfusion to remove circulating bilirubin and antibody
Begin phototherapy at 460–490 nm instead of transfusion
Administer IVIG to block reticuloendothelial Fc receptors
Transfuse a full-unit volume of non-irradiated RBCs rather than a small-volume aliquot

questions.explanation_correct: Anemia of prematurity results from a developmentally blunted erythropoietin response to falling hemoglobin in the neonate, compounded by immature organ systems; it presents with tachycardia, tachypnea, poor weight gain, and increased oxygen requirement, as in this infant. The source-directed management for symptomatic anemia in this setting is a small-volume RBC aliquot (10–15 mL/kg) from a dedicated donor unit to limit donor exposure, and irradiation is mandatory because the infant weighs less than 1200 g, to prevent TA-GVHD. The remaining options are therapies specific to HDFN/hyperbilirubinemia or violate neonatal transfusion volume and irradiation requirements, not appropriate management of isolated anemia of prematurity.

option_explanations.A: Correct: small-volume aliquots (10–15 mL/kg) from a dedicated unit minimize donor exposure, and irradiation is mandatory for premature neonates weighing less than 1200 g to prevent TA-GVHD.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: double-volume exchange transfusion is used to remove bilirubin and antibody in severe HDFN/hyperbilirubinemia, not for isolated anemia of prematurity without immune hemolysis.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: phototherapy (460–490 nm) conjugates bilirubin to treat neonatal jaundice; it does not raise hemoglobin or address anemia of prematurity.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: IVIG blocks Fc receptors to reduce antibody-mediated hemolysis in HDFN; this infant's anemia is due to a blunted erythropoietin response rather than immune-mediated RBC destruction.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect: neonatal transfusion should use small-volume aliquots (10–15 mL/kg), and irradiation is required for infants under 1200 g to prevent TA-GVHD; a full, non-irradiated unit violates both principles.

questions.teaching_point: Anemia of prematurity is driven by a blunted erythropoietin response in the immature neonate (not immune hemolysis) and presents with tachycardia, tachypnea, poor weight gain, and increased oxygen need; management uses small-volume aliquots (10–15 mL/kg) from a dedicated donor unit, irradiated if the infant weighs under 1200 g. This must be distinguished from HDFN-specific therapies (phototherapy, IVIG, exchange transfusion), which target bilirubin/antibody-mediated hemolysis rather than isolated anemia of prematurity.

source_references.supports: Neonates have a blunted EPO response to anemia; anemia of prematurity manifests as tachycardia, tachypnea, poor weight gain, or increased oxygen requirement.
Simple neonatal transfusions use small-volume aliquots (10–15 mL/kg) from dedicated units to minimize donor exposure.
Irradiation is mandatory for IUT, exchange transfusion, and premature neonates under 1200 g to prevent TA-GVHD.
Phototherapy, IVIG (to block Fc receptors), and double-volume exchange transfusion are listed as HDFN-directed neonatal therapies for bilirubin/antibody removal.
Neonatal transfusion logic corroborates small-volume aliquot dosing (10–15 mL/kg) and irradiation for TA-GVHD prevention.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old man with relapsed multiple myeloma is scheduled to receive high-dose melphalan chemotherapy followed by autologous hematopoietic progenitor cell (HPC) transplantation. His own peripheral blood stem cells were mobilized, collected by apheresis, and cryopreserved weeks earlier for reinfusion after conditioning. A resident asks the transfusion medicine attending why autologous HSCT is generally considered to carry the lowest risk of immune-mediated complications compared with allogeneic HSCT.

options.text: The recipient and the graft originate from the same individual, so there is no HLA disparity to trigger graft-versus-host disease or graft rejection
The apheresis product is treated with pathogen inactivation before cryopreservation, eliminating alloreactive donor T cells
ABO compatibility between the graft and the recipient is confirmed before collection, preventing a hemolytic complication
The HPC product is irradiated before infusion, eliminating any immunocompetent lymphocytes capable of causing graft-versus-host disease
Because the product is intended for autologous use only, it is exempt from infectious disease testing requirements

questions.explanation_correct: Autologous HSCT uses the patient's own previously collected and cryopreserved stem cells, so the "donor" and recipient are genetically identical. Because there is no HLA disparity between graft and host, the immunologic mechanisms that drive complications in allogeneic HSCT — graft-versus-host disease (donor T cells attacking recipient tissue) and graft rejection (host immune response against a foreign graft) — cannot occur. This is precisely why the source material identifies autologous HSCT as carrying the lowest risk of immunologic complications, in contrast to allogeneic HSCT, which carries GVHD, passenger lymphocyte syndrome, and rejection risk tied to HLA and donor-recipient antigenic differences.

option_explanations.A: Correct. Donor and recipient are the same person in autologous HSCT, eliminating the HLA disparity that underlies GVHD and graft rejection in the allogeneic setting; this is why autologous transplant carries the lowest immunologic risk.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. HPC grafts are not subjected to pathogen-reduction/inactivation processing as a routine step, and this is not the reason autologous transplants avoid immune complications — the protection comes from donor-recipient identity, not ex vivo product treatment.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect distractor. ABO matching is not essential for HPC donor selection at all (HPCs do not express ABO antigens and will not trigger hyperacute rejection), so 'confirming ABO compatibility' is neither the mechanism performed nor the reason autologous grafting is low-risk.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect and a classic board trap in the opposite direction: the HPC graft must NEVER be irradiated, because irradiation would destroy the stem cells' proliferative capacity. Irradiation is reserved for cellular blood components given to the patient, not the HPC product itself.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect distractor. Autologous HPC/blood products are not exempt from infectious disease testing; testing is still required, for example when the product is shipped to another facility.

questions.teaching_point: Autologous HSCT is immunologically the lowest-risk transplant modality because donor and recipient are the same person, eliminating the HLA disparity that drives GVHD and graft rejection in allogeneic HSCT — distinguish this from unrelated board traps: ABO matching is not required for any HPC graft, the HPC product itself must never be irradiated (only post-transplant blood components for the patient are irradiated), and autologous products are not automatically exempt from infectious disease testing.

questions.stem:
A 34-year-old woman with acute myeloid leukemia in first complete remission has a healthy monozygotic (identical) twin sister who is willing to donate peripheral blood stem cells. The transplant team proceeds with a syngeneic HPC(A) transplant following myeloablative conditioning.

Which of the following is the most accurate statement about the biology of this transplant compared with a standard allogeneic HSCT from an HLA-disparate donor?

options.text: Both GVHD and the graft-versus-neoplasm (GVN) effect are expected to be diminished, because the donor and recipient are genetically identical and lack the HLA disparity that drives each process
The HPC graft should be irradiated before infusion to eliminate residual donor lymphocytes and further reduce GVHD risk
Donor and recipient must be ABO-identical for the transplant to proceed safely, since HPCs express the same ABO antigens found on mature red cells
The graft should be leukocyte-reduced before infusion to decrease the number of immunocompetent donor cells transferred
Because there is no risk of graft-versus-host disease, this recipient will not need irradiated or pathogen-reduced blood components after transplant

questions.explanation_correct: The source defines the major histocompatibility barrier (HLA disparity) as the primary driver of both graft-vs-host disease and graft rejection, and states that the graft-versus-neoplasm effect specifically arises when donor immune cells recognize and attack residual tumor cells in allogeneic transplants. A syngeneic graft, by definition, comes from a monozygotic twin who is genetically identical to the recipient. With no immunologic/HLA disparity between donor and recipient, the immune-mediated processes that underlie both GVHD and the GVN effect are expected to be diminished — this is the classic trade-off taught for syngeneic transplantation: lower GVHD risk but also loss of the graft-versus-tumor benefit that depends on donor-recipient immunologic difference.

option_explanations.A: Correct: syngeneic (identical twin) donor-recipient pairs lack the HLA disparity that the source identifies as the driver of both GVHD and the GVN effect, so both are diminished.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect — this is the classic 'irradiation error' board trap: the HPC graft itself must never be irradiated, since irradiation destroys the stem cells' ability to proliferate and engraft.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect — the source explicitly states HSCT does not require ABO identity because HPCs do not express ABO antigens, unlike solid organ transplants.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect — the source explicitly states the HPC graft should never be leukocyte reduced, regardless of GVHD concerns.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect — the source states HSCT recipients must receive irradiated or pathogen-reduced blood components for life to prevent transfusion-associated GVHD from residual blood product lymphocytes; this recommendation is tied to HSCT recipient status generally, not eliminated simply because the graft itself is syngeneic.

questions.teaching_point: In syngeneic (identical-twin) transplantation, the absence of HLA/genetic disparity between donor and recipient diminishes both GVHD and the graft-versus-neoplasm effect, since both depend on immunologic recognition of disparate tissue — this is distinct from other HSCT-wide safety requirements (never irradiate/leukoreduce the graft, no ABO identity needed, lifelong irradiated blood products for TA-GVHD prevention) that apply regardless of donor type.

questions.stem:
A 52-year-old woman with high-risk AML in first complete remission lacks an HLA-identical sibling donor; her only full sibling was typed and found to be HLA-mismatched, which is consistent with the expected pattern of haplotype inheritance in siblings. The transplant program therefore initiates a search of the unrelated donor registry. Once a promising unrelated candidate is identified, the histocompatibility laboratory must perform typing on both the patient and the donor candidate before the donor can be finalized.

Which of the following best describes the required approach to HLA typing in this unrelated-donor evaluation?

options.text: Serologic (antigen-level) typing of HLA-A and HLA-B is sufficient, since these are the only loci relevant to donor selection.
High-resolution, DNA-based molecular typing at the allele (two-field) level should be performed at HLA-A, -B, -C, -DRB1, and -DQB1.
Because HPCs do not express HLA antigens on their surface, HLA matching is no more essential to donor selection than ABO matching.
A physical crossmatch mixing donor cells with recipient serum must be completed before the molecular HLA typing results can be interpreted.
Typing only needs to cover HLA-DR and HLA-DQ, since these Class II loci are the ones expressed on antigen-presenting cells.

questions.explanation_correct: For unrelated donor selection, the sources specify that high-resolution, DNA-based typing at the allele (two-field) level is mandatory, and that the target loci for unrelated donors are HLA-A, -B, -C, -DRB1, and -DQB1 — this is the level of resolution needed to safely match an unrelated pair given the extreme polymorphism of the HLA system.

option_explanations.A: Incorrect. Antigen-level serologic typing is not sufficient for unrelated donor selection; the sources require high-resolution DNA-based (allele-level) typing. It is also incomplete, since HLA-C, -DRB1, and -DQB1 must also be typed, not just -A and -B.
option_explanations.B: Correct. The sources state that high-resolution DNA-based typing at the allele level (two-field) is mandatory for unrelated HSCT donor selection, with HLA-A, -B, -C, -DRB1, and -DQB1 as the target loci for unrelated donors.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. This distractor misapplies the true statement that HPCs do not express ABO antigens (so ABO matching is not required for graft acceptance) to HLA, which is the opposite of what the sources teach: HLA compatibility is described as the paramount factor for engraftment and GVHD prevention in allogeneic HSCT, and Class I HLA molecules are expressed on most nucleated cells.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. The sources describe a virtual crossmatch as comparing the recipient's known HLA antibody profile against the donor's documented HLA type specifically to predict compatibility without a physical mixture of cells and serum; DNA-based HLA typing is a molecular method (SSOP, SSP, or NGS) that does not require an antecedent physical crossmatch to be interpreted.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect. While HLA-DR and -DQ (Class II) are correctly noted as restricted to antigen-presenting cells, limiting typing to these loci omits the Class I loci (HLA-A, -B, -C) that are also required targets for unrelated donor typing.

source_references.supports: High-resolution DNA-based typing at the allele level (two-field) is mandatory for unrelated HSCT donor selection; also supports the 25% sibling HLA-match rule from linkage disequilibrium and that HLA compatibility is paramount for engraftment/GVHD prevention.

questions.domain: 17. Blood Bank/Transfusion Medicine-Specific Administration and Laboratory Management
questions.stem:
A hospital-based blood bank's quality manager investigates a deviation. A technologist performed antibody screening on a unit of red blood cells using a lot of antisera reagent before the lot's required day-of-use quality control had been performed and documented — a clear deviation from the facility's cGMP-based process control requirements. The lapse was discovered later the same shift during a routine review of QC logs. The affected unit had not yet been released from the blood bank; it was immediately quarantined, the reagent lot was pulled from use, and the unit was destroyed. The unit was never issued to any patient or shipped to another facility. The quality manager documents the event, performs a root cause analysis, and implements a corrective action.

Which of the following best explains why this event, as described, does not meet the FDA's reporting criteria for a Biological Product Deviation (BPD)?

options.text: Reagent quality control lapses fall outside the scope of cGMP oversight, so they never qualify as manufacturing deviations.
The affected unit was never distributed — it remained within the facility's control and was destroyed before release — so the criterion requiring an already-distributed product was not met.
The event does not require reporting because it was discovered and corrected within 45 days, the FDA's allowed window for resolving deviations without notification.
Because the quality manager's investigation classified the event as warranting a corrective action rather than labeling it an accident, external BPD reporting is not triggered.
Since no patient received the unit, the event is a near-miss, and near-miss events are, by definition, exempt from any documentation or investigation requirements.

questions.explanation_correct: FDA BPD reporting requires that an event meet all five criteria simultaneously: it is associated with manufacturing, represents a deviation from cGMP/specifications, may affect safety/purity/potency, occurred while the facility had control of the product, AND involves a product that has been distributed (left the facility's control). Here the reagent QC lapse is a genuine cGMP process-control deviation that occurred while the facility had control of the unit, but because the unit was quarantined and destroyed before release, the "distributed" criterion is never satisfied. Since all five criteria must be met for a BPD report to be required, this event does not trigger FDA BPD reporting — though it must still be documented, investigated, and corrected internally as a nonconforming event under the facility's QMS.

option_explanations.A: Incorrect. Process control, including performance of required reagent QC (e.g., day-of-use QC for antisera), is itself a core cGMP/QMS element; failing to complete it before use is precisely the kind of manufacturing deviation cGMP is meant to prevent.
option_explanations.B: Correct. BPD reporting requires all five criteria to be met, including that the product has been distributed (left the facility's control). Because the unit was quarantined and destroyed before release, this criterion fails, so no BPD report is required even though a real cGMP deviation occurred.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. The 45-calendar-day window is the deadline for submitting a BPD report once an event that already meets all five reporting criteria has been discovered — it is not a grace period that excuses reporting if the deviation is corrected quickly.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. Whether a BPD report is required depends on whether the event objectively meets the five defined criteria (manufacturing-associated, cGMP deviation, potential safety/purity/potency impact, facility control, and distribution), not on how the facility's internal corrective/remedial action is labeled.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect. A near-miss or nonconforming event is not exempt from documentation or investigation — it must still be captured and investigated under the QMS; the reason no BPD report is owed here is specifically the unmet distribution criterion, not a blanket exemption for near-misses.

questions.teaching_point: FDA Biological Product Deviation (BPD) reporting under cGMP requires that ALL FIVE criteria be met simultaneously — manufacturing association, cGMP/specification deviation, potential impact on safety/purity/potency, facility control at time of occurrence, and prior distribution of the product (it left the facility's control). A genuine cGMP process-control deviation caught and contained before release does not trigger mandatory FDA BPD reporting, because the "distributed" criterion is unmet — but it must still be documented, root-caused, and corrected internally as a nonconforming event under the facility's QMS.

questions.domain: 17. Blood Bank/Transfusion Medicine-Specific Administration and Laboratory Management
questions.stem: A blood bank technologist is working the evening shift when she notices that a colleague from another hospital department was recently admitted as an inpatient. She has no role in that patient's care. Purely out of personal curiosity, she uses her own valid laboratory information system login to look up the colleague's ABO/Rh type and antibody screen results, and later mentions the results to a coworker in the break room. Which of the following legal/regulatory principles has this technologist most directly violated?

options.text: Negligence — deviation from the standard of care that causes patient injury
Battery — performing a procedure without obtaining patient consent
HIPAA Privacy Rule — unauthorized access to and disclosure of protected health information
Doctrine of Informed Consent — failure to disclose risks, benefits, and alternatives before transfusion
Strict liability — liability for providing a defective blood product regardless of fault

questions.explanation_correct: HIPAA governs the privacy and security of a patient's protected health information (PHI). Blood bank and hospital records (laboratory results such as ABO/Rh type and antibody screen findings) must be protected from unauthorized access as part of HIPAA compliance, and information management systems are required to protect the confidentiality of both electronic and paper patient data. Here, the technologist had no care-related reason to view the colleague's results, accessed them anyway, and then disclosed them to a third party — this is an unauthorized access/disclosure of PHI, i.e., a HIPAA Privacy Rule issue, not a treatment-related tort or consent failure.

option_explanations.A: Negligence is failure to follow established standards of care (e.g., a testing or labeling error) that leads to patient injury. No testing error or patient harm is described here — the issue is inappropriate access to and disclosure of results, not a deviation in technical care.
option_explanations.B: Battery is defined as performing a transfusion (or other procedure) without obtaining any consent. No transfusion or procedure was performed on anyone in this scenario, so battery does not apply.
option_explanations.C: Correct. HIPAA governs privacy and security of PHI, and records must be protected from unauthorized access as part of HIPAA compliance. The technologist accessed and disclosed a patient's laboratory results without any care-related justification, which is an unauthorized access/disclosure of PHI.
option_explanations.D: The Doctrine of Informed Consent requires clinicians to explain the risks, benefits, and alternatives of a transfusion to the patient before the procedure. No transfusion decision or consent discussion is involved in this scenario.
option_explanations.E: Strict liability is a doctrine (rarely applied to blood) holding a provider liable for a defective product regardless of fault. No blood product or product defect is at issue here.

questions.teaching_point: HIPAA Privacy Rule specifically addresses unauthorized access to and disclosure of protected health information, and must be distinguished on boards from other medicolegal doctrines governing transfusion practice — battery (no consent for a procedure), negligence (deviation from standard of care causing injury), informed consent (failure to disclose risks/benefits/alternatives before transfusion), and strict liability (defective product regardless of fault).

questions.domain: 17. Blood Bank/Transfusion Medicine-Specific Administration and Laboratory Management
questions.stem: A hospital-based transfusion service performs ABO/Rh typing, antibody detection, and compatibility (crossmatch) testing, then issues red blood cell units to patients for transfusion. The service does not collect blood from donors and does not process, manufacture, or further modify components; all units are obtained from a separately FDA-licensed community blood center. During a regulatory affairs review, the laboratory director is asked to characterize this transfusion service's obligation to register as a blood establishment with the FDA. Which of the following best describes the facility's regulatory status?

options.text: It must register with the FDA and hold its own Biologics License Application (BLA), because it administers previously licensed blood products to patients.
It must register with the FDA as a blood establishment but does not need a BLA, because it never distributes components in interstate commerce.
It is exempt from FDA establishment registration, but it remains subject to CMS/CLIA regulation and may still be inspected by the FDA for cause.
It is exempt from all federal and regulatory oversight because it does not collect, process, or manufacture blood components.
It must obtain AABB accreditation as a legal prerequisite before it is permitted to perform compatibility testing.

questions.explanation_correct: Blood is regulated by the FDA as both a drug and a biological product, and FDA establishment registration is generally mandatory for blood establishments; a Biologics License (BLA) is required specifically for entities distributing blood in interstate commerce. However, the sources call out a specific, testable exception: transfusion services that are limited to crossmatching and transfusing (i.e., no collection or processing) are exempt from FDA establishment registration. This exemption does not remove all oversight — such facilities remain subject to CMS/CLIA regulation and can still be subject to FDA "for-cause" inspections. This scenario describes exactly that kind of facility (testing and issuing only, no collection/processing), so option C is the accurate description.

option_explanations.A: Incorrect. Registration and BLA obligations attach to blood establishments that collect, process, or distribute components; a transfusion service that only tests and administers already-licensed product does not itself need to register or hold a BLA — this is precisely the misconception the source flags as a board trap.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. This inverts the actual exemption: the source states transfusion services limited to crossmatch/transfuse are exempt from FDA registration itself, not merely exempt from the BLA while still needing to register.
option_explanations.C: Correct. Matches the source directly: crossmatch/transfuse-only services are exempt from FDA registration but remain subject to CMS/CLIA oversight and FDA for-cause inspection.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. Overstates the exemption — the source explicitly notes the facility 'remain[s] subject to CMS/CLIA and for-cause FDA inspections,' so oversight is not eliminated entirely, only FDA establishment registration is waived.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect. The sources describe AABB as an accreditation agency that performs periodic inspections (like CAP), not as a body whose accreditation is a legal prerequisite substituting for FDA registration/licensure requirements.

questions.teaching_point: Blood establishment FDA registration is generally mandatory, and licensure (BLA) is required for interstate distribution — but transfusion services limited to crossmatching and transfusing (no collection/processing) are specifically exempt from FDA registration, while still remaining subject to CMS/CLIA regulation and FDA for-cause inspection authority.
